To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any funding criteria applied to the £200,000 allocated to the National Churches Trust via the Historic Environment Fund; (ii) the body responsible for scoring applications, and distributing funding to churches; and (iii) any process by which the funding criteria and body were determined.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an annual breakdown of any churches that have received funding from the National Churches Trust via the Historic Environment Fund, since 2018.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of applications submitted for the £200,000 funding for the National Churches Trust via the Historic Environment Fund; (ii) the number of successful applications, broken down by constituency.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) where the £200,000 for the National Churches Trust via the Historic Environment Fund was advertised; and (ii) any steps that were taken to promote awareness of the fund.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with special educational needs who will be placed on a reduced timetable due to a lack of sufficient available places that meet their acute needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is actively engaging with schools to confirm the placement of pupils with special educational needs, and work to increase specialist provision across the school estate continues. Given the significant amount of work currently under way, any contingencies needed to minimise disruption to learning will depend on the outcomes of both the placement process and construction programmes. This is being kept under continuous review.If/Where contingencies are required, EA will support schools to develop and implement appropriate arrangements to enable continued access to education.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has taken on whether the current placement process for the 2026-2027 academic year complies with statutory duties relating to SEN provision, including timely and appropriate placement.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) any work carried out with the Department of Education on a strategy to end vaping on school grounds; and (ii) the number of times he has met with the Minister of Education, since October 2024, on the issue of vaping on school grounds.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether any of the churches receiving funding through his Department's Historic Environment Fund have also received money for their facilities through the Community Infrastructure Fund.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail when he will publish the findings from the consultation on the draft Childcare and Early Learning Strategy.
answer_text: The public consultation on the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy closed on 24 March 2026 with 468 responses received. My officials are currently undertaking a comprehensive analysis which will be used to inform the final version of the strategy. While it is difficult to estimate when this exercise will be complete, I can confirm publication of the outcomes of the analysis will be made publicly available in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) any work carried out with the Department of Health on a strategy to end vaping on school grounds; (ii) any plans to facilitate resources and training to aide teachers and school staff to reduce the number of young people vaping, particularly on school grounds; and (iii) the number of times he has met with the Minister of Health on the issue of vaping on school grounds.
answer_text: There is no work ongoing with the Department of Health to develop a strategy to end vaping on school grounds. All schools have a statutory responsibility within their scheme of management (Articles 3-6 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998) for developing their own policies to promote good behaviour and discipline. There is a DE Circular which encourages smoke/vape free environments, which can be found on the department’s website (DE Circular 2014/25 ) and a further DE Circular on Drugs Guidance which states that 'e-cigarettes are treated in the same way as ordinary cigarettes and their use is prohibited on school premises', this can also be found on the department’s website (DE Circular 2015/23). I have not met with the Minister of Health to specifically discuss the issue of vaping on school grounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps to be taken to ensure the Education Authority and his Department work with families to find flexible pre-school placements that best meet the needs of our children.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for managing the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) and for ensuring that sufficient funded pre-school education provision is available in all areas to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it.  DE plays no role in this process.The pre-school admissions process is preference based, however, the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) is not designed to provide every target age child with a funded place in their first preference setting, nor is it possible to do so.At close of Stage One of the 2026/27 pre-school admissions process on 23 March, 98.81% of children who applied were offered a funded pre-school education place in a setting of their parents’ preference, with 92.43% of all applicants receiving an offer at their parents’ first preference setting.Sufficient pre-school provision remains available to meet demand across Northern Ireland with 3,031 funded pre-school education places still available at 23 March to accommodate the small number of children that had not yet received an offer of funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference at close of Stage One. Parents of children who have not yet received an offer of a funded pre-school education place are encouraged to submit further preferences in Stage Two and remain with the admissions process to the end to provide the best opportunity of their child receiving an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.  In each of the last twelve years at least 99.8% of children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school education place, and remained with the admissions process to the end, received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents choosing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the impact of the unavailability of hydrocortisone sodium phosphate 100mg/1ml injections for patients with Addison’s disease.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and marketing authorisation holders (MAHs) have a legal obligation to inform DHSC of any anticipated shortages or discontinuations across the UK within six months or as soon as reasonably practicable. My Department works closely with DHSC and was aware of an initial disruption affecting hydrocortisone 100mg/1ml solution for injection in early 2025 and the recent decision to withdraw from the UK market.In relation to any assessments of the impact of non-availability of this product, all medicine supply disruption notifications reported to DHSC are assigned a risk tier from 1 to 4, dependent on patient and system impact in accordance with agreed systems and processes. DHSC undertook a risk assessment in line with normal shortage management processes, and my Department worked with our partners across the UK on a national management plan utilising well-established tools and processes, where applicable. This included providing management advice to healthcare professionals on the actions they could take via a Medicine Supply Notification and information published on the NHS Special Pharmacy Service (SPS) medicines supply tool, which is updated regularly by DHSC and provides up-to-date information on medicine disruptions.I recently became aware of concerns raised by patients with Addison’s Disease relating to the alternative product, hydrocortisone 100mg powder for injection. These concerns were in relation to the reconstitution of the alternative powdered product during an adrenal crisis emergency and the campaign to make a hydrocortisone auto-injector available. Any decision for such a medicinal product to be made available for the UK market is a commercial one for the pharmaceutical industry. A MAH would need to go through the appropriate regulatory processes with the UK medicines regulator to obtain a marketing authorisation for the UK market. However, my Department is aware that DHSC is continuing to work with industry to discuss what further can be done to find a longer-term solution. Any updates on the management advice will be communicated to HSC healthcare professionals via the SPS medicines supply tool.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41699/22-27, to detail any steps he took to secure the healthcare support required to enable special school summer schemes to proceed this summer.
answer_text: Officials indicated a number of weeks ago that challenges around inter alia, on-site nursing provision may have prevented summer schemes from proceeding in 2026.  Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority liaised with the Department of Health to address concerns regarding on-site nursing provision to support the safe running of Special School Summer Schemes for 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any alternative provision which will be put in place for children with complex needs who would have attended special school summer schemes this year.
answer_text: Officials indicated a number of weeks ago that challenges around inter alia, on-site nursing provision may have prevented summer schemes from proceeding in 2026.  Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority liaised with the Department of Health to address concerns regarding on-site nursing provision to support the safe running of Special School Summer Schemes for 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will extend the £4.5 million Pre-school Inclusion Fund into the 2026-2027 financial year.
answer_text: As you will be aware, no Executive Budget has yet been agreed for 2026/27.  Current indications are that Education is facing a projected funding gap of c£0.6 billion in 2026/27, increasing to c£0.9 billion in 2028/29.  Funding decisions for the 2026/27 financial year will be taken following confirmation of my Department’s 2026/27 final opening budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when he was first made aware that a lack of nursing provision could prevent special school summer schemes from taking place in 2026.
answer_text: Officials indicated a number of weeks ago that challenges around inter alia, on-site nursing provision may have prevented summer schemes from proceeding in 2026.  Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority liaised with the Department of Health to address concerns regarding on-site nursing provision to support the safe running of Special School Summer Schemes for 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the allocation of the £4.5 million Pre-school Inclusion Fund, with a view to moving it from a financial year basis to an academic year funding model.
answer_text: Departmental budgets are set on an annual for financial years basis, ending 31 March.  Committing funding on an academic year basis would require certainty over future year budgets at the start of the academic year in September.  In the absence of agreed, realistic multi-year budgets this has not been possible.  I am therefore only able to provide funding on an individual financial year basis.Notwithstanding this constraint, I remain fully supportive of the Pre-school inclusion fund, which is entirely funded from earmarked Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy funding.  However, continuation of this fund is dependent on sufficient Executive ELC budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on whether the British Government will increase tax-free childcare above the current rate of 20 per cent.
answer_text: There have been a number of representations over this last two years, to the British Government, firstly by my predecessor and then by myself, calling for an increase to the current 20 per cent TaxFree Childcare topup and the associated cap. TaxFree Childcare is a reserved tax matter, with any changes considered as part of British Government fiscal events. The Autumn Budget 2025 did not announce any changes to TaxFree Childcare, and the existing 20 per cent topup and associated caps therefore remain in place. However, the Budget did include wider measures to support families, including the removal of the twochild limit in Universal Credit from April 2026 and increases to Universal Credit entitlements alongside childcare support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage increase in the average cost of childcare since the childcare subsidy scheme was introduced.
answer_text: My Department collects fee information from childcare providers registered for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS). Average fees vary by provider type and by the age of the child; all figures presented are based on fees charged by providers before any deductions through Universal Credit, NICSS or TaxFree Childcare.At September 2025, data submitted by NICSS registered providers shows the average daily fee across all provider types for children aged 0-4 was £43. The majority of children attend day nurseries, therefore these fees provide the most representative indication of typical childcare costs. The average daily fee in private day nurseries across Northern Ireland for children aged 0-4 was £60, compared with £50 in community and voluntary day nurseries. Combined, day nurseries charged an average of £58 per day. Analysis of private day nurseries shows between September 2024 and September 2025, the average daily fee for children aged 0-4 increased by around 9%, rising from £55 to £60. This is lower than the previously reported 12%, which reflected the change between September 2024 and April 2025 and was based on a smaller dataset. The table below summarises the average fees for children aged 0-4 at September 2025, broken down by constituency, based on fees submitted by NICSSregistered providers.Average Daily Fee, by constituency – September 2025ConstituencyDay Nursery (Private)Day Nursery (Community and Voluntary)Day Nursery (All)All Provider TypesNorthern Ireland £60£50£58£43Belfast East£65£49£62£45Belfast North£63£42£54£45Belfast South£64£48£59£47Belfast West£64£49£55£49East Antrim£62£50£61£41East Londonderry£57£56£56£42Fermanagh and South Tyrone£59£58£59£42Foyle£62£45£59£43Lagan Valley£59£53£59£43Mid Ulster£54£53£54£40Newry and Armagh£55N/A£55£42North Antrim£58£69£59£41North Down£62£54£61£45South Antrim£62N/A£62£43South Down£55N/A£55£42Strangford£57£47£56£44Upper Bann£60£58£60£42West Tyrone£56£51£54£42Separately, NICSS was expanded in September 2025 to include children aged 0-11. September 2025 data shows that average fees for school-age childcare was £6 per hour, £13 for a morning session and £20 for an afternoon session. I am aware that providers have implemented further price increases from April 2026, and my Department is currently collecting updated data to monitor this.It is important to note that annual fee increases have always been a factor, are not unique to Northern Ireland and are driven by wider factors affecting the sector, including staffing costs and inflationary pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his consideration to introduce a cap on childcare fees.
answer_text: As part of the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, my Department undertook scoping and analysis of how fee capping models operate in practice, including the funding, regulatory and delivery mechanisms required to support them. There would be significant cost, legislative and operational implications associated with such an approach. For these reasons, the draft Strategy clearly stated that the capping of fees is not currently proposed but will be kept under consideration. The position remains unchanged.As an Executive Strategy, any future decision on fee capping in a market that is predominately privately operated will require collective Executive agreement and the appropriate Executivelevel funding commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her plans for safer journeys to school.
answer_text: My Department has been developing a whole-system programme of measures, including education, engineering, enforcement and legislation. Building on extensive engagement with stakeholders, I have now launched a public consultation on proposed changes to legislation that will improve the safety for children and other passengers, when boarding or alighting a bus.The consultation outlines a preferred legislative approach that would restrict the overtaking of all stationary buses in clearly defined dangerous circumstances. It also seeks views on wider options to address the broader risks associated with vehicles passing buses.Alongside this, officials continue to progress work across education, behavioural change, enforcement readiness, and targeted infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total amount invested by his Department in the £70 Million Queen's Parade Regeneration Project in Bangor.
answer_text: My Department has invested over £10 million on site acquisition and ongoing maintenance costs for the Queen’s Parade Regeneration Project in Bangor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of any additional funding his Department has contributed to projects supported through the Belfast City Region Deal.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on reinstating onsite nursing provision in special schools.
answer_text: The provision of nursing staff within education settings is a matter for the Department of Health and the corresponding local Health and Social Care Trust.Officials from my Department and Department of Health, along with representatives from the Education Authority and the Public Health Agency (PHA), have commenced work to take forward the recommendations of the PHA review of population health needs of children with complex medical needs attending special schools. This cross-departmental work will include the development of a new model of care for special schools that meets the needs of children. In turn, this will determine the level of resourcing required across professions, including nursing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when special schools will get confirmation of their summer schemes.
answer_text: It is expected that special schools will be notified about summer schemes before the end of March 2026.Any decision on running summer schemes in special schools is an operational one for the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the rationale for the decision to remove Irish language from the logo on his Department’s website.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail which statutory organisation holds responsibility for each aspect of the implementation of the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol.
answer_text: The Housing Executive holds responsibility for the implementation of the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol however this is not a statutory duty

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department will take to ensure that homelessness organisations and charities receive timely and reliable notice of future activations of the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“When the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP) is triggered the Housing Executive notify funded homelessness service providers at the earliest opportunity to arrange additional accommodation and support for the duration of the SWEP. If needed, we will also engage with other providers who may be able to provide additional capacity.  We work with our service providers to identify people sleeping rough and provide them with accommodation and support while also ensuring our social media channels provide details of relevant contact numbers for the Housing Executive, Welcome Organisation and First Housing. Any groups seeking information regarding SWEP can get reliable and relevant information from our social media or by contacting our out of hours service for assistance.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any organisations that receive direct notification from his Department when the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol is activated.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive works with a range of providers to source additional emergency crash provision, including Depaul, Extern, Legion of Mary, Salvation Army, and Simon Community, which can range from additional short-term provision within hostels, or rest centres which act as a congregate crash facility and is facilitated by Welcome Organisation.In respect of direct engagement with rough sleepers our focus will be on engaging with those organisations directly funded by the Housing Executive where the Housing Executive is responsible for ensuring that appropriate safeguarding procedures are in place via any applicable contract management arrangements. Therefore, we will notify these providers directly as well as posting on our social media to inform those in the sector that the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol has been activated.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of confusion experienced by homelessness organisations and frontline charities, on 16 February 2026, regarding activation of the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any reason for discrepancies in pay and conditions for classroom assistants who work in a Specialist Provision in Mainstream schools, and those who work in Special Schools.
answer_text: Each SEN Classroom Assistant post is aligned to one of two grades - Classroom Assistant SEN who are on the NJC pay scale 11-14 or Classroom Assistant Additional SEN who are on the NJC pay scale 17-20.To meet the Additional Special Needs Classroom Assistant rate, a Classroom Assistant must be working with a pupil(s) who hold(s) a Statement of Special Educational Needs. Then, in working with that pupil, the Classroom Assistant should meet at least one of the first three points on the Additional Special Needs job description namely:Undertake more comprehensive or invasive medical/clinical procedures.Help pupils with specialist communication skills and/or sensory difficulties access the curriculum.Deal with pupils with very challenging behaviour as identified by the Educational Psychology Service as requiring additional provision.As the majority of pupils placed in special schools have significant and complex needs, the number of classroom assistants on the Additional Special Needs Classroom Assistant rate in special schools is higher than that of specialist provisions attached to mainstream schools.

This includes Joint Negotiating Council meetings and the Pay, Terms and Conditions and HR Policy Central Forum.  Departmental officials attend these regular meetings.

Opportunities for people to engage with arts and cultural activities.		The potential to build a legacy of knowledge, resources, or relationships that can support future activity. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the closure of the kitchen at St Brigid’s Primary School, Downpatrick, due to low staffing levels, is temporary.
answer_text: The recent disruption to the Education Authority’s (EA) school meal service in the Downpatrick area has primarily resulted from a critical staffing shortage in the main production kitchen at St Brigid’s Primary School, which supplies meals to other educational settings. This challenge reflects the broader recruitment and retention difficulties currently affecting the EA as well as the wider catering and hospitality sector, making it increasingly difficult to fill vacancies and provide adequate cover for staff absences.As a temporary measure, retired staff have been re-engaged by the EA to support the hot meal service for all affected schools in the Downpatrick area. This arrangement is intended to remain in place until a permanent staffing solution is secured, but it is not guaranteed and may change at short notice.The EA continues to implement targeted recruitment initiatives to attract new personnel to the school meals service. Additionally, the EA Catering Service deploys mobile catering staff to support schools as needed; however, recruitment for these mobile roles remains challenging and current staffing levels are insufficient to meet demand.When staffing issues arise, such as those experienced in South Down, the EA Catering Service reviews several contingency plans. These include transporting hot meals from alternative production kitchens, delivering pre-prepared meals from approved suppliers, or, if no staff are available to heat and serve meals, providing packed lunches as a last resort.The EA is responsible for managing the catering service as an operational matter. The Head of EA Catering, in collaboration with senior catering managers, has operational responsibility for the delivery of school meals across Northern Ireland and determines the most appropriate response in accordance with Health and Safety Food Regulations. The Head of EA Catering has engaged with my officials on this matter and there has therefore been no direct correspondence between the EA Chief Executive and myself.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether additional funding has been allocated to address special educational needs placement shortfalls for September 2026.
answer_text: The Education Authority has made available an additional financial and support package for schools who develop Specialist Provision in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any funding allocated to address special educational needs placement shortages for September 2026.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to assess and develop the accommodation solutions required to facilitate SEN placements for September 2026.  My SEN capital investment programme has a dedicated budget to ensure that the funding needed to deliver this accommodation is prioritised.In addition to the package already offered by the Education Authority (EA) which includes centrally financing teaching and non-teaching costs from the EA budget, an initial furniture and equipment budget circa £20k associated with any set up works required and a lump sum of £3k per class to purchase resources; for 2026 new specialist provisions are receiving:Whole school learning and development offer - 20% of teaching staffin mainstream and up to five classroom assistants can avail of specific SEN training with up to five days release to be completed between April and June 2026.SENCo release - one month for stabilising Specialist Provision embedding processes/workload in addition to a further one day per month per class.Administrative support - £10k to be provided to each school to cover additional administrative tasks associated with the creation and maintenance of specialist provisions.Principal Champions - To release five Principals who have implemented Specialist Provisions to provide support, advice and guidance to new schools (one Post Primary, four Primary)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) a breakdown of (a) grammar; and (b) non-grammar, schools approached by the Education Authority to increase special educational needs capacity for September 2026; and (ii) the number of schools in each sector that agreed to (a) increase special educational needs capacity; or (b) establish a specialist provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:To date, in planning for September 2026 and beyond, 47 Secondary Schools and 27 Grammer Schools have accommodated further exploratory discussions with EA officers. These have led to the intention of 20 projects in the post primary sector (18 Secondary Schools and two Grammer Schools). In addition, to this work approximately 300 mainstream school leaders in areas experiencing significant pressure for SEN places were invited to eight engagement sessions in October and November 2025. Of the 70 Post Primary school leaders invited (33 Secondary Schools and 37 Grammar Schools), 19 attended – nine Secondary Schools and ten Grammar Schools. There has been further engagement with these schools on an individual level to explore the possibility of further solutions and EA continue to seek ways to encourage more schools to become involved.Ensuring the right provision is available for children with SEN across all communities is a societal responsibility which cannot be achieved without system wide support and long term financial investment in the school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the flooding in Newcastle in October 2025, to outline any work his Department has carried out on drainage in Donard Forest to reduce the risk of future flooding.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service has conducted an assessment of the drainage infrastructure following the flooding incident at Donard Forest and has undertaken necessary remedial works due to the exceptional water flow during the storm. These works included removing excess material from roadside drains deposited due to the flooding and increasing flow capacity of drainage network where appropriate within the forest. Additional flood mediation measures have been installed across forest roads at frequent intervals to ensure that heavy rainfall is channelled into the drainage network to avoid surface washing and subsequent blockages. The forest road in particular that runs adjacent to a section of Sunningdale Drive has been raised to increase the roadside drain holding capacity to prevent overspill into private residence property.Forest Service is also in the process of engaging with NIE to explore options to increase the waterflow capacity of the drain that traverses NIE occupied land and acts as the outlet from the forest in the vicinity of Sunningdale Drive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Ulster University Magee campus.
answer_text: I was pleased to attend the launch of the Magee Expansion Taskforce OneYearOn Update Report at Ulster University’s Magee campus on 14 January. This report sets out the clear progress delivered over the past year as we work towards the Executive commitment of expanding the campus to 10,000 students.Amongst other things, the report highlights the significant achievements to date, including that all the land required for expansion is secured, that over £27 million in capital investment has been delivered, and that new teaching and learning facilities - such as the Timber Quay computing labs - are already benefiting students. Additionally, student numbers have grown to 6,505, a 22% increase since the Executive was restored, with strong demand reflected in a 21% rise in applications for 2025/26.The next steps outlined will include continued capital development and establishing an expert working group to address student accommodation challenges.While this oneyear update demonstrates that momentum is strong, challenges remain. I will continue to prioritise the Magee expansion within my Department’s budget and work closely with Executive colleagues and local partners to ensure progress continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any engagement he has undertaken with businesses in the aquaculture sector affected by the ongoing blockage on access to seed; and (ii) any steps he intends to take to reassure local enterprises that his Department will work to protect and support small rural businesses within the sector.
answer_text: My Department has not prohibited any seed movements within the past year. On 25 November 2025, seven licences were issued under Section 15 of the Fisheries Act 2020 permitting vessels to fish for seed mussel (Mytilus edulis) at the Rusk Channel for relaying at licensed aquaculture sites in Northern Ireland. To date 351 tonnes of mussel seed has been relayed. Regular engagement with industry stakeholders is undertaken through existing stakeholder forums, including Aquaculture NI meetings and the Bottom-Grown Mussel Consultative Forum. My officials have attended these meetings, provided updates and discussed concerns raised directly with business owners. My Department is currently developing a biosecurity protocol with associated guidance for aquaculture movements of shellfish and will engage with aquaculture businesses on the protocol before finalising and publishing it.Small rural businesses are a vital part of our economy and rural communities. They will be an important consideration in future aquaculture policy development. Any potential impacts will be assessed through regulatory, rural needs and equality impact assessments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the Minister for Agriculture, Food and the Marine regarding invasive non-native species (INNS) risk assessment and biosecurity planning for shared waterways, in the last 18 months; (ii) how this engagement has shaped the INNS risk assessment and biosecurity plan; and (iii) when his Department intends to publish the INNS biosecurity plans.
answer_text: My officials have been engaging with their counterparts in the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine and Bord Iascaigh Mhara (BIM) with regards to Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS) risk assessments carried out for movements of marine shellfish species.This engagement has helped to shape biosecurity guidance for aquaculture movements of marine shellfish that is being developed by my officials. The guidance includes risk assessment and biosecurity plan templates aimed at reducing the risk of introducing or spreading INNS and has considered the approach taken in neighbouring administrations in its development.My officials intend to share this guidance with industry stakeholders for feedback in January 2026 before finalising and publishing in Spring 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to remove the current blockage on the movement of oyster seed from reputable hatcheries, which has been in place for the past year; and (ii) when he expects this restriction to be lifted.
answer_text: My Department has not prohibited any oyster seed movements during the past year; therefore, there is no restriction to be lifted.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale behind restricting mussel farmers’ access to wild seed fisheries, given the Agri-Food and Bioscience Institute’s stated assessment of the potential benefits this access may have in helping to control invasive species.
answer_text: As one of the UK’s fisheries policy authorities, my Department is required to apply the fisheries objectives set out in the Joint Fisheries Statement (JFS) to manage fisheries in the Northern Irish marine area and balance environmental, social and economic factors in all decision making.My decision not to open the Northern Irish seed mussel fishery in 2025 was based on a review of all available evidence and advice as well as consideration of my Department’s legal obligation to take proactive steps to reduce the risk of introduction and/or spread of Invasive Non-Native Species. Additionally, Northern Irish registered vessels that applied to take part in the 2025 all-island seed mussel fishery have been granted seed mussel licences by my Department in November 2025 to avail of the seed fishery in the Rusk Channel, Republic of Ireland. Subsequent movement requests were also authorised for movements of mussel seed to Northern Irish licensed aquaculture sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether he is aware that aquaculture operators in South Down have been unable to transport seed into the area for the past year; (ii) any actions his Department is taking to ensure that the aquaculture sector can continue to operate sustainably; and (iii) his assessment of the economic impact this is having on local communities and on the long-term viability of the sector.
answer_text: My Department has not prohibited any seed movements within the past year. On 25 November 2025, four licences were issued under Section 15 of the Fisheries Act 2020 permitting vessels to fish for seed mussel (Mytilus edulis) at the Rusk Channel for relaying at licensed aquaculture sites in Carlingford Lough. Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS) can pose a significant economic risk to the aquaculture industry should they become established. My officials have advised operators that shellfish movements must be accompanied by an INNS risk assessment and biosecurity plan, and each movement request follows the same process to ensure a robust, science-based decision can be made while preventing adverse economic and environmental impacts. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any communication his Department has had with (i) the Irish Government; and (ii) relevant cross-border agencies, concerning the preservation and long-term sustainability of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department has not had any communication with either the Irish Government or relevant cross-border agencies concerning the preservation and long-term sustainability of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre (TNOC). However, I remain committed to constructive engagement with all relevant stakeholders where opportunities arise to support TNOCs future and ensure its continued contribution to outdoor recreation and sporting development in Northern Ireland.  Sport NI has primary responsibility for the management of TNOC and is currently undertaking a Strategic Review, including a planned consultation exercise with the sector. Sport NI has advised me that as part of this process they have engaged with Mountaineering Ireland and informed Sport Ireland about the forthcoming consultation. While they have stated that they have no plans for direct consultation with cross-border organisations, Sport NI has indicated that it would welcome contributions from any such organisation wishing to participate in the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of occasions on which he has met with Sport NI regarding Tollymore National Outdoor Centre; and (ii) the associated records and minutes of those meetings.
answer_text: While responsibility for the management of TNOC lies in the first instance with Sport NI, officials from my Department have been regularly engaging with Sport NI since TNOCs closure.Sport NI are in the process of carrying out a Strategic Review of operations at TNOC.  A public consultation exercise is due to commence shortly with the review due to be completed by Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) methodology used to estimate the £45 million in savings associated with the UK Government's Fraud, Error and Recovery Bill; and (ii) assumptions underpinning this figure.
answer_text: The UK Government expects that the Public Authorities (Fraud, Error and Recovery) Bill will deliver total benefits of £1.5 billion over the next five years.  Based on a 3% read across, the Northern Ireland benefits are estimated at £45 million over a five-year period.  This assumes that Northern Ireland will introduce a parity Bill with the same measures contained in the UK Government’s Bill. A draft Executive paper was shared with Executive Colleagues on 29/9/25 to agree to the addition of a Northern Ireland Fraud, Error and Recovery Bill to the Executive Legislation Programme.  This has not yet been included on the Executive agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the proposed review of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre; and (ii) to detail the anticipated date for its completion.
answer_text: Sport NI has primary responsibility for the management of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre, making this an operational matter for the organisation. Sport NI has advised it expects the review will be completed by Spring 2026.Officials from my Department will continue to engage with Sport NI throughout the review process.Sport NI is bringing on board a service provider to engage the sector and wider public in the reimagining of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre and aims to begin consultation this month with a range of engagements happening with the sector and options presented to the public.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the key benefits of the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework sets out a clear roadmap towards a changed society where women and girls are free from all forms of violence, abuse and harm. We are already seeing positive activity taking place across a wide range of community settings through the Local and Regional Change Fund, including in youth groups, sports organisations, schools, businesses and the arts sector. The Power to Change Campaign, jointly launched by TEO, DoJ and the PSNI earlier this year, targets harmful attitudes and behaviours of men and boys.Materials from this campaign will further be utilised in the ‘Fair Game’ initiative which uses the platform of sport to challenge harmful attitudes and create safe environments for young people both on and off the pitch. The deputy First Minister and Junior Minister Reilly were delighted to take part in the launch of this programme at Windsor Park. And our new pilot campaign on coercive control addresses the issue of ‘how it makes you feel’, enabling women and girls to identify what coercive control is and the impact it has.The First Minister recently visited Ballinderry Community Hub and met with a group of young people who are benefitting from this initiative. It was great to see how this work is making a real difference, helping to change attitudes and create safer communities.Together we can create a better society, where violence against women and girls is never accepted and everyone is safe and free from harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with the Government in the south of Ireland in regard to the development of the Blueprint for Bovine TB Eradication; and (ii) any all-island interventions being considered to collaboratively eradicate Bovine TB .
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication identified regionalisation and partnership working as key strategic priorities. The report built upon the recommendations within the Chief Veterinary Officer’s Review of bTB in Northern Ireland and the previous bTB Eradication Strategy. All of these have been informed by our regular engagement and cooperation with, and assessment of policies being progressed by, colleagues in the Republic of Ireland. In October I was pleased to announce, alongside Martin Heydon TD, Minister of Agriculture, Food and the Marine in the Republic of Ireland, an innovative, cross-border regionalisation pilot project which will aim to assist both jurisdictions in tackling the growing impact of bTB. Shared Island funding has now been confirmed, and officials have been extensively engaging in developing the pilot for deployment next year.Both Departments will jointly implement a range of measures on a coordinated regional basis, with the aim of reducing bTB incidence and transmission. The roll out of the pilot and the assessment of how well the measures deployed within it are at reducing disease, will be consistent with, and contribute to, both Departments’ policies and approaches to working with farmers and key stakeholders to eradicate bTB.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment on when Bovine Tb will be eradicated in (i) the North; and (ii) across the island of Ireland.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication sets out clear targets for the eradication of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland (NI). The aim of the TBPSG, working with DAERA, is to reduce bTB herd incidence by 2% by 2030, placing NI on a pathway to halve current bTB levels by 2040 and achieve eradication by 2050. This is an ambitious target and progress achieved towards this target will become clearer over the time ahead as new measures (as outlined in the Blueprint) are implemented and become part of the wider TB Programme. In relation to the wider island of Ireland, I cannot comment on the eradication targets set out by the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (DAFM) in the Republic of Ireland.  DAFM has recently published a new TB Action Plan which sets out its current priority actions for tackling this challenging disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's work to reduce restrictions on accessing Seasonal Worker visas for the fishing sector.
answer_text: UK immigration policy is an excepted matter, so I cannot deliver changes to the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme. In early 2024, the UK Government confirmed that fishing and fish processing roles are non-seasonal and ineligible for this visa route. The Migration Advisory Committee’s July 2024 review also advised against extending the scheme. I subsequently wrote to MAC Chair Professor Brian Bell, stressing the need for a labour solution for the fishing sector until automation becomes viable.		I have raised Northern Ireland’s agri-food and fisheries labour challenges with the Defra Secretary of State, the Home Secretary and the Minister for Migration and Citizenship, providing feedback on the Immigration White Paper and reiterating proposals from the NI Fishermen’s Federation.		In September 2025, I wrote to Defra’s new Secretary of State highlighting concerns about the impact of UK immigration changes on Northern Ireland’s agri-food businesses, which rely on seasonal and skilled labour. I stressed the operational challenges these changes create and called for urgent attention to ensure labour availability for sectors such as fishing and food processing. The Secretary confirmed that while the Seasonal Worker visa route is extended to 2030 for horticulture and poultry, fishing remains excluded. The UK Government committed instead to reduce reliance on overseas labour through domestic upskilling, apprenticeship levy reforms and engagement with devolved administrations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her assessment of the effectiveness of the flood alleviation plans for Downpatrick; and; (ii) a timeframe for the completion of the works.
answer_text: Installation of pumps on the Quoile River was assessed as an option as part of the Downpatrick Flood Alleviation scheme feasibility study, published in June 2025. A couple of options using pumps were considered within the flood model. However ,the model indicated that none of the options would significantly reduce the risk of flooding to Downpatrick, and as a result  pumping options are not being considered further.Overall the Feasibility Study has identified viable solutions to significantly reduce flood risk to over 100 properties within Downpatrick. Following the publication of the feasibility study, further work has commenced to develop the business case for the potential flood alleviation scheme. This work is on programme and is currently expected to be completed by Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 34319/22-27 and AQW 34323/22-27, of the approximate 9% equivalent to £900,000 of total subsidy funding paid out between April 2025 and October 2025 used to offset the increase in childcare fees, to detail (i) any measures the Department implemented to help offset these increases; or (ii) were the resulting savings the normal outworking of the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: The £900,000 paid through the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is attributed to increases in childcare fees by providers since April 2025. It has no connection to the increase in the subsidy cap. Fee increases are a normal part of business practice in the sector and in anticipation of this, I increased the maximum subsidy cap by 10% (£167 to £184), from April to ensure that parents who were already close to the £167 cap continued to receive the full 15% subsidy, even as their costs increased.My department does not have a role in setting or regulating childcare fees. While the NICSS subsidises childcare costs for families, it is not intended to fully insulate parents from all inflationary pressures, including those arising from UK-wide changes to the Minimum Wage and Employers National Insurance.The NICSS provides a 15% reduction on a parent’s bill, meaning that when providers increase their fees, the overall subsidy contribution also increases so   that families continue to benefit from the NICSS as intended.If the NICSS had not been in place, the expected fee increases from April 2025 would still have been applied, putting even greater pressure on the affordability of childcare for many families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to, AQW 34319/22-27 and AQW 34323/22-27, to detail the percentage increase of childcare fees in the private childcare sector, excluding the community and voluntary sectors, since the introduction of the Childcare Subsidy Scheme in September 2024.
answer_text: Analysis of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) data shows that childcare fees have increased by approximately 12% on average, between September 2024 and April 2025. This increase is the same when looking specifically at the private childcare sector, excluding community and voluntary providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a scoping exercise has been carried out to extend urgent care in the Downe Hospital to alleviate pressures in emergency services at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: Downe Hospital Urgent Care Centre has a key role in delivering unscheduled care services to the population of Downpatrick and its surrounding area.  No scoping exercise has been carried out to further extend Urgent Care at the Downe Hospital.Some consideration is, however, being given to widening the range of treatments that that can be managed by the Urgent Care Centre in the Ulster Hospital and extending the Urgent Care Centre’s hours there.  These changes should improve patient flow in the Emergency Department (ED) and allow more patients to be seen more quickly there as well as the Urgent Care Centre. The Trust has been asked to submit a business case for the proposed developments with a view to extending the urgent care services next year subject to available funding.

answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a review of legislation is being considered to reduce the national speed limit, to align with the reduction of speed limits in other areas, such as the Republic of Ireland and Scotland.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. My Department is intending to launch a public consultation in the coming weeks on a Draft Speed Limit Review that will gather evidence from across these islands and Europe on a number of speed related matters, including the national speed limit.  My Department will consider the outcomes of this consultation that will inform a final Speed Limit Review Report which will be published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that will be taken to secure Laser Protection Advisors to assist in Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority pre-registration inspections.
answer_text: I am advised that RQIA has been actively engaging with Regional Medical Physics Service to secure access to laser protection adviser expertise to assist in undertaking pre-registration inspections, and that the necessary support has now been sourced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration her Department is giving to a dedicated portal, where members of the public can submit dash cam footage of dangerous driving, allowing the police to follow up directly.
answer_text: In terms of policing operations, these decisions are ultimately a matter for the Chief Constable of the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB). PSNI is operationally independent from me, as Justice Minister, therefore it is important that I respect the operational independence of the Chief Constable, and the role of the NIPB in holding him to account.I suggest that you may wish to contact the PSNI directly for further information.  I am aware that the PSNI have developed a portal for members of the public to submit their digital evidence in relation to an incident, such as Dash Cam footage. The public must report the incident first via an online report or by phoning one of the PSNI contact centres, so that they can assess it as to the prioritisation of their response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the average time users have spent on the InnerDrive online learning platform; and (ii) any evaluation of engagement, to date.
answer_text: A full evaluation of the programme is being undertaken by the Education and Training Inspectorate and a Post Project Evaluation will also be conducted.  My officials will consider next steps informed by all relevant evidence in due course. As of 11 November 2025, 9,858 teachers have registered and have spent 19,205 hours on the programme. 4,165 online professional learning courses have been completed, some as part of whole-school training and completed by multiple teachers but included only once in the figure above. These courses are also supported by a range of resources including Teaching Unpacked, Playlists, Student Lessons and the Cognitive Science Network.A total of 161 schools have signed up for the Teacher CPD Academy and as a condition of participation schools were required to incorporate the professional learning into whole-school planning, utilise one school development day to reflect on the programme, ensure attendance at all live webinars and participate in an evaluation of the programme. InnerDrive has provided an overview of engagement to date which will inform the overall evaluation and are supporting school professional development leads with their strategies for their schools, including how to further boost engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost of (i) surveys; (ii) consultations; and (iii) evaluations, commissioned by his Department, in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place for (i) when the InnerDrive contract expires in May 2026; and (ii) evaluation; and (ii) renewal.
answer_text: A full evaluation of the programme is being undertaken by the Education and Training Inspectorate and a Post Project Evaluation will also be conducted.  My officials will consider next steps informed by all relevant evidence in due course. As of 11 November 2025, 9,858 teachers have registered and have spent 19,205 hours on the programme. 4,165 online professional learning courses have been completed, some as part of whole-school training and completed by multiple teachers but included only once in the figure above. These courses are also supported by a range of resources including Teaching Unpacked, Playlists, Student Lessons and the Cognitive Science Network.A total of 161 schools have signed up for the Teacher CPD Academy and as a condition of participation schools were required to incorporate the professional learning into whole-school planning, utilise one school development day to reflect on the programme, ensure attendance at all live webinars and participate in an evaluation of the programme. InnerDrive has provided an overview of engagement to date which will inform the overall evaluation and are supporting school professional development leads with their strategies for their schools, including how to further boost engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current accuracy rate for the Interferon Gamma blood test; and (ii) the number of cases requiring retesting in (a) 2021-22; (b) 2022-23; (c) 2023-24; and (d) 2024 to date.
answer_text: The current accuracy rate for the Interferon Gamma blood test Interferon gamma (IFN-g) testing is a widely used diagnostic test approved by the World Organisation for Animal Health and approved by the EU for use in bovine tuberculosis (bTB) eradication programmes. In Northern Ireland it is used in bTB breakdown herds as an ancillary test, along with the Comparative Intradermal Tuberculin Test (known as the skin test), to help identify reactors at an earlier stage of infection that may be missed by the skin test.  No diagnostic test is perfectly accurate, and accuracy is measured in terms of the sensitivity and specificity of the test.Sensitivity is the probability that a test will correctly identify those with the disease i.e. true positives. The higher the sensitivity, the lower the probability of incorrectly classifying an infected animal as uninfected (a false negative result). The sensitivity of the IFN-g test has been estimated at ~ 90%, meaning that on average the test may miss one in ten true positive animals.  Specificity is the probability that a test will correctly identify an animal that is free from infection as negative. The higher the specificity, the lower the probability of incorrectly classifying an uninfected animal as infected (a false positive result). The specificity of the IFN-g test has been estimated at ~ 96.5 percent, which in practical terms means that on average 3-4 false positives per 100 disease-free animals tested. The number of cases requiring retesting in (a) 2021-22; (b) 2022-23; (c) 2023-24; and (d) 2024 to dateThe IFN-g test is offered on a voluntary basis to herds that meet the selection criteria. The outcome of the test is either positive or negative, there are no inconclusives. Retesting of individual samples only occurs when a sample has failed quality controls. Reasons for this include sample identification issues, insufficient volume of blood or damaged sample tubes.The numbers of individual samples retested for each year were:2021-22: 02022-23: 22 samples2023-24: 02024-to date: 2 samplesOn a herd basis, herds are usually only offered IFN-g testing once during a disease breakdown, but in some exceptional cases the disease situation may warrant a second test. To date all testing has been voluntary, including repeat testing in a herd. The number of herds retested per year were: 2021–22: 9 herds2022–23: 7 herds2023–24: 1 herd2024 to date: 12 herds
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the TB Blueprint for Eradication is expected to come into effect.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication (the Blueprint) came into effect on its publication on 15 April 2025.  	I was pleased to endorse the Blueprint which represents the culmination of extensive work by the TBPSG, drawing on the wide breath of members’ knowledge, experience and expertise. This document, and the work now required, represents a new, bold step forward for government-stakeholder cooperation in the fight against bovine TB maintenance and spread. The Blueprint sets out the key priorities and actions to be further developed to tackle this disease and clearly identifies those measures where progress must be made in the immediate future across the three pillars of people, cattle and wildlife. I am committed to progressing new innovative measures and initiatives to tackle this disease as quickly as possible and my officials continue to work closely with TBPSG to implement the Blueprint.  New departmental structures are in place to progress this work, with initial focus on the new public consultation on possible wildlife intervention options; the increased use of interferon gamma blood testing; ensuring TB skin testing is carried out to the highest standard; the delivery of on-farm biosecurity advice; and work on the cross-border regionalisation initiative.  A number of the new proposals will require additional resources while others will need public consultation or new or amending legislation.  It is clear that only by working together can we tackle all of the factors that contribute to the spread and endurance of this costly disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to promote breeding practices that improve cattle immunity and overall herd health.
answer_text: My department commenced delivery of the Bovine Genetics Project (BGP) earlier this year. This is one of the measures being progressed through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme and is being delivered in partnership with industry through Sustainable Ruminant Genetics (SRG). 	This project will provide herd owners with the data and evidence to make more informed breeding decisions, including genetic information on a wide range of animal health traits. 	My department, together with SRG, will be delivering a suite of knowledge transfer and innovation programmes to ensure herd owners have the necessary knowledge and understanding to implement breeding practices which lead to improved animal health and disease resistance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration has his Department given to the role and operation of bTB finishing farms.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication included an action for my department to review its criteria for the approval and establishment of Alternative Control Herds (ACH).  Such criteria have been in place here since 2012, however, to date, no ACH has yet been established.    		This work is underway and is focusing on considering what barriers have prevented uptake along with consideration of a number of legislative requirements that might affect the criteria for setting up such herds.  These include European rules around moves from infected establishments and herd testing; and domestic legislation regarding compensation payments for cattle compulsorily slaughtered for the control of bovine TB. 		Officials have had a number of discussions with the TB Partnership Steering Group on this matter in recent months and I expect to receive advice on potential options shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider allocating places on the TB Partnership Steering Group, to on-the-ground farmers to ensure their views and practical experiences are represented.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG) is made up of farming, industry, veterinary and environmental stakeholders including: •	Association of Veterinary Surgeons Practising in Northern Ireland•	Dairy Council for Northern Ireland•	Livestock & Meat Commission for Northern Ireland•	Northern Ireland Agricultural Producers’ Association•	Northern Ireland Badger Group•	Northern Ireland Livestock Auctioneers’ Association•	Northern Ireland Meat Exporters’ Association•	North of Ireland Veterinary Association•	Ulster Farmers’ Union•	Ulster Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals•	Ulster WildlifeThe Group promotes shared ownership, responsibility, co-ordination and direction setting - ultimately creating the drive and concerted effort needed for progressing disease eradication in close cooperation with my department.  I consider that those groups and organisations represented on the TBPSG are highly effective conduits for ensuring that the views and experiences of their respective memberships, including those of farmers, are fully taken into account.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage increase of childcare fees, since the introduction of the Childcare Subsidy Scheme in September 2024.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average daily rates charged by childcare providers in (i) December 2023; (ii) September 2024; (iii) March 2025; and (iv) October 2025.
answer_text: From September 2024, all childcare providers registered to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) have been required to submit up-to-date fee information, which has provided a consistent and reliable data set to track childcare costs for the first time. No information is held prior to this date.While there are significant variations in fee structures and increases by providers across the sector, analysis of the NICSSS data shows that the average daily fees increased from £53 in September 2024 to £57 in April 2025, representing a 7% increase. This analysis is based on daycare settings, which represent 83% of the total subsidy payments made since April 2025.Analysis is ongoing and will take account of further information available following the expansion of the scheme to school age children with effect from 1 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the criteria for inclusion in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will be reviewed (i) to recognise the value of unpaid labour; and (ii) to include a broader range of occupations, such as student nurses and social workers.

The NICSS is aligned with His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs (HMRC) Tax-Free Childcare scheme and is based on hours worked and income thresholds, not occupation. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to (i) detail the increase in average childcare fees from (a) 2010-2021; and (b) 2021-25; and (ii) compare the rate of increase across these two periods.
answer_text: From September 2024, all childcare providers registered to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) have been required to submit up-to-date fee information, which has provided a consistent and reliable data set to track childcare costs for the first time. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding from the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, that has been absorbed by childcare provider fees, since the scheme's introduction.
answer_text: Analysis of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) data shows that childcare fees have increased by approximately 12% on average, between September 2024 and April 2025. This analysis is based on daycare settings, which represent 83% of the total subsidy payments made since April 2025.This increase was not unexpected given that providers increase their fees annually, however, wider inflationary and wage pressures across the childcare sector from April 2025 are likely to also have had an impact. In anticipation of these fee increases I increased the monthly subsidy cap for the scheme by 10% in April 2025 and raised the administrative payments to providers. While increases in fees are determined by individual childcare providers, the NICSS subsidy of 15% on childcare invoices has acted to reduce the impact of these increases. Analysis of the NICSS data indicates that approximately 9% (£900,000) of total subsidy funding paid out between April 2025 and October 2025 has been used to offset these increases.I acknowledge that childcare costs for some families remain unacceptably high and addressing affordability will continue to be a key focus of the forthcoming draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I will bring to my Executive colleagues in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether having separate referral pathways for statutory and non-statutory early years settings for Early Years Local IMPACT Team support, risks creating disparities in access to early intervention support for children.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Non-statutory providers access EA Special Educational Needs support services through Community Paediatrics/Allied Health Professional referral to the Educational Psychology Service (EPS) who then refer into the Local Impact Teams (LITs). The EPS, through their Health and Social Care Trust triage arrangements ensures early identification of children to the EA.Once a child is referred via this route, they receive the same form and level of support from LITs as those referred from statutory preschool settings.  Therefore, having separate referral pathways for statutory and non-statutory early years settings for Early Years Local IMPACT Team does not affect the level of support delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for maintaining the non-statutory referral route for early years support, through Community Paediatrics and Educational Psychology Services, rather than utilising the new Early Years Local IMPACT Teams for statutory early years providers.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Education and Library Board SEN services were originally designed and scoped to only support grant-maintained preschool settings where the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice (CoP) applies fully.  As non-statutory providers do not uniformly apply the SEN CoP, their route to access EA SEN support services has remained predominantly via Community Paediatrics/Allied Health Professional referral to the Educational Psychology Service who then refer into the Local Impact Teams (LITs).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria used by the Local IMPACT Teams Support Moderation Referral Panel, in the support overview scoring guide for allocations.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised:The Moderation panel does not use criteria.  The Request for Involvement (RFI) form and supporting information submitted by the school is considered by the moderation panel using the support pathway framework. The Support Pathway Framework is a flexible framework that supports staff to confidently apply pathways that match SEN Categories of Need. It is proportionate, needs-led and part of a graduated approach. Child, family and school factors as well as Graduated Response evidence are considered as part of a holistic and child-centred approach to each RFI. There is no definitive list of factors that automatically influence decision-making, instead, each RFI is considered individually, with the child’s unique circumstances reviewed in the context of the full RFI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work undertaken by their Department under the auspices of the North South Ministerial Council.
