To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering the reinstatement of full emergency services at the Downe Hospital.
answer_text: My Department is not considering the reinstatement of full emergency services at Downe Hospital.  As previously outlined in recent Assembly questions and correspondences, the Downe Hospital provides a vital role in providing unscheduled care services other than type 1 Emergency Care.The consolidation of the Trust’s emergency care at Dundonald ensures that services remain safe and sustainable for the South Eastern population.  The provision of emergency care at the Downe Hospital is neither safe nor sustainable without 24 hour-7 days- a-week rapid diagnostics and full resuscitation facilities and associated services.  These are all provided at Dundonald.  Prior to the Trust’s decision to consolidate Emergency Care at the Ulster Hospital, it was unable to fill a number of long-term consultant vacancies which led to a reduction in opening hours initially and eventually the development of a clinically appropriate Urgent Care Centre.It should also be noted that the current hospital configuration whereby the population of South Eastern Trust has one acute hospital with emergency and scheduled care on one site has been supported by a number of reviews over the last two decades.  The Trust’s configuration ensures that services remain safe and sustainable for its population.More recently, at the start of this year, my Department publicly consulted on ‘Creating A Network for Better Outcomes’ which proposes a network of hospitals and their services to be provided in each locality.  This outlined the core services of Regional Centres, General Hospitals, Area Hospitals and Local Hospitals across the HSC.The Outcome report of the consultation is expected later this year but proposes that the Ulster Hospital remains an area hospital for the South Eastern HSC Trust.  Although the Downe Hospital does not have an ED, it continues to deliver a broad range of local hospital services and regional day procedure services.  The Trust is fully committed to providing safe and sustainable services in the Downe Hospital for its local population.  Trust colleagues, along with Department officials, continue to explore innovative and sustainable approaches to further enhance local services within available resources.  New sustained investments have already been put in place over the last number of years securing a range of services in the Downe Hospital and local area, including a more stable permanent workforce within the Urgent Care Centre, expanding frailty services delivered within the Acute Frailty Unit and a new ‘Hospital at Home’ model. In delivering these services, the Trust also acknowledges the excellent care delivered by its staff in the Downe Hospital and in the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any scoping exercises his Department has undertaken to utilise the Downe Hosptial to reduce delays in ambulance handovers at Emergency Departments.
answer_text: Improving ambulance handover times remains a key Departmental priority.Earlier this year Departmental officials, alongside clinicians from Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), visited a hospital in London to learn from their approach to releasing ambulances more quickly and, following that visit, Trusts and NIAS were asked to adopt a similar model.To support this, £12 million has been allocated to Trusts to implement targeted interventions by the end of October 2025.  The immediate goal is to eliminate ambulance delays over two hours by 1 December 2025 and all Trusts signed up to this goal at a workshop held in August 2025. Once the initial milestone is achieved, we will move towards meeting the 15-minute handover standard.Ambulance handover delay is a system-wide challenge and I have tasked my Department with leading the co-ordinated action needed to deliver the required improvements.With regards to the Downe Hospital, you will be aware that the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has consolidated its type 1 emergency care services on the Ulster Hospital site, to ensure that services remain safe and sustainable for the Trust’s population. This means that the Downe Hospital does not have the ability to provide type 1 emergency care services. However, the Downe Hospital does still have a role to play as a type 3 Urgent Care Centre, helping to ensure that type 1 EDs are kept for the most serious emergencies, which is a key measure in our attempts help manage demand at type 1 EDs, including the subsequent impact on ambulance wait times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any plans his Department has to reduce delays in ambulance handovers at Emergency Departments, in order to ensure that ambulances are not left waiting for extended periods.
answer_text: My Department recognises the challenges faced by Emergency Departments (EDs) and has worked closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays. To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has implemented a range of targeted initiatives. These include the expansion of Urgent Care Centres, Phone First Services and Minor Injury Units, offering patients timely alternatives to ED attendance.Ahead of the coming winter, my department has worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and Trusts to ensure that an additional 18 emergency medicine consultants have been appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland. A further 10 consultants are expected to complete their training by December 2025, and it is anticipated that they will also be recruited locally to provide further resilience and senior decision-making capacity to our EDs this winter.In 2025/26, I am determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle waiting times in EDs. That is why I initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. I attended the first of these events on 4 March 2025 and was encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from HSC leaders across the system.The workshop saw attendance from a diverse group of stakeholders within our system, including Royal Colleges, clinicians, and other frontline services. All our system leaders are engaged in this initiative.I hope to publish my Winter Preparedness Plan in the coming weeks that will aim to see improved outcomes before the next winter.  The plan will identify other steps that my Department and key partners are taking to help manage pressures.In August, SPPG held a regional workshop with senior management and clinicians from Trusts and NIAS, focusing on improving ambulance handover times. Input was provided from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital in London where they have been successful in reducing ambulance handover delays. SPPG are working with NIAS and Trusts to finalise Ambulance Handover guidance, based on the experience of the London model. Incremental improvement targets for Phase 1 have been agreed with Trusts that by 1 December there will be no ambulance handover delays in excess of two hours outside an Emergency Department, whilst also ensuring there no deterioration in delays in excess of 15 mins, 30 mins and 60 mins. Once the 2-hour milestone is achieved, we will move towards meeting the 15-minute standard.All Trusts have committed to putting measures in place to improve ambulance handover delays.Ultimately, all of these measures are aimed at improving flow throughout the hospital, reducing ambulance handover times and congestion in our EDs and improving waiting times.These efforts are part of a broader system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care, both within EDs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any steps her Department is taking to support early intervention as part of youth justice services for children and young people.
answer_text: AQO 2513 22-27 - ANSWER I launched the Strategic Framework for Youth Justice in 2022.  A key aim of this Framework is to divert children from the youth justice system at the earliest possible opportunity.    The Youth Justice Agency leads a number of Earlier Stage Diversion schemes, taking referrals primarily from PSNI for children at the cusp of, or involved in, earlier stages of offending. These schemes aim to help children address their offending behaviour before it escalates into formal entry into the criminal justice system.  Earlier Stage Diversion is delivered either directly by YJA or through targeted small grant funding to community-based organisations.    Working in this way has helped reduce the number of children appearing in Court and entering custody.  This has been recognised by Criminal Justice Inspection NI who in a 2024 report on Youth Interventions said that “the work of the Youth Justice Agency and its staff has been pivotal in reducing the number of children prosecuted in the Youth Court with some ending up in custody.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline when he intends to bring forward the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy to the Assembly.
answer_text: I will bring forward the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.  The Strategy will cover a 10-year horizon and implementation will be subject to Executive approval and agreement to release the additional funding required.  I will engage with Assembly colleagues on the draft Strategy, following agreement by the Executive. The Strategy will have the needs of the child at its core; and in line with my department’s vision, it acknowledges that a child-led, play-based approach is essential in both formal and informal settings, recognising that high-quality play experiences are especially important for children from disadvantaged backgrounds or with additional needs. Early learning and childcare investment to date has funded commissioned research and significant data collection, including:data collected as part of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) - on childcare cost and usage;   RSM Review of Childcare Services in Northern Ireland - providing analysis of supply and demand, affordability, workforce challenges, and barriers to employment, particularly for women and low-income families;the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency Childcare Survey results; and the Department for the Economy research report on Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support.      Recommendations and analysis from the research and data collected to date, alongside the extensive stakeholder engagement has informed the development of the draft Strategy for Executive consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline when recommendations within the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will be implemented.
answer_text: I will bring forward the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.  The Strategy will cover a 10-year horizon and implementation will be subject to Executive approval and agreement to release the additional funding required.  I will engage with Assembly colleagues on the draft Strategy, following agreement by the Executive. The Strategy will have the needs of the child at its core; and in line with my department’s vision, it acknowledges that a child-led, play-based approach is essential in both formal and informal settings, recognising that high-quality play experiences are especially important for children from disadvantaged backgrounds or with additional needs. Early learning and childcare investment to date has funded commissioned research and significant data collection, including:data collected as part of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) - on childcare cost and usage;   RSM Review of Childcare Services in Northern Ireland - providing analysis of supply and demand, affordability, workforce challenges, and barriers to employment, particularly for women and low-income families;the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency Childcare Survey results; and the Department for the Economy research report on Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support.      Recommendations and analysis from the research and data collected to date, alongside the extensive stakeholder engagement has informed the development of the draft Strategy for Executive consideration.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration given to the impact of the revised special diet policy on inclusion and equality of access to education for children with additional needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that its policy for the provision of special diets has not changed since 2014. The EA does not require a pupil to have a confirmed medical diagnosis for a special diet to be provided for the pupil. The EA policy continues to require a relevant medical professional, examples of which include the child’s general practitioner, dietician or speech and language therapist, to document that the pupil has a medical need for a special diet. It is only necessary for this to be documented once, subject to any subsequent change in the child’s special dietary requirements. This ensures that the meal prepared by the EA is in line with the child’s dietary requirements as documented by the relevant medical professional. It also ensures a child’s special dietary requirement is based upon a medical need and not only on a child’s personal food preferences. Upon completion of the child’s special diet form and provision of the documentation from a relevant medical professional, a meeting involving the child’s parent(s) or carer, school leadership and school-based catering staff is held to agree the particular catering arrangements for that child. The outcomes agreed are documented to ensure the special diet is catered for accurately and consistently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a timeline for (i) when construction of the new build at Knockevin Special School, Downpatrick will begin; and (ii) when it will be completed.
answer_text: My Department, is working with the Education Authority to identify a suitable site for the major capital project for Knockevin School.The procurement of the Integrated Consultancy Team is already underway and nearing completion.  The Project Board has been established and is receiving regular updates with the project still on programme for a proposed start on site date in 2030.  My officials will continue to work with the Education Authority to identify a suitable site for the new school. It is important to note that these timelines remain subject to the necessary business case approvals and the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the review conducted by the Chief Nursing Officer in relation to ensuring there is on-site nursing provision at special schools.
answer_text: I have received the final report from the Public Health Agency (PHA) which detailed the outcome of their review of the population health needs of children attending Special Educational Needs (SEN) schools in Northern Ireland.The report highlights significant regional variations in the provision of health care for children with complex needs in SEN schools. It is clear that further work is required to ensure equitable and timely access to appropriate healthcare services for all children, regardless of location.A number of areas have been identified where immediate improvements are required. I have therefore asked my Chief Nursing Officer to work closely with policy leads in the Department and other key stakeholders to progress the report’s recommendations.A copy of the report has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans to reinstate the on-site nurse at Knockevin School, Downpatrick.
answer_text: Following a visit from Departmental and South Eastern HSC Trust officials to the Knockevin Special School earlier this year, there has been significant engagement between the Trust and the school. Community Children’s Nursing staff provide oversight of the complex needs of children in the School, with on-site Nursing cover available two mornings every week.  This model has been welcomed and well evaluated by everyone.  The Community Children’s Nurses have completed training within the school, to ensure that staff have the knowledge and skills to meet the assessed needs of individual children. A further visit to the school by the Trust team is planned for early October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12 will be published.
answer_text: The first step in the review, a public consultation exercise on key issues for the childcare sector, closed on Friday 20 June 2025. A total of 495 responses were received and a consultation report is currently being drafted by my officials.If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers.A Review Reference Group of stakeholders will also be considering the wider Minimum Standards in detail and assisting with the drafting of a refreshed set of Standards, on which I intend to consult in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline the reasons for the Downe Hospital Emergency Department transition from a Type 2 to a Type 3 facility; and (ii) to detail what consultation took place, including with which stakeholders his Department and the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust engaged with prior to implementation.
answer_text: (i)	The Consultant led service, which the South Eastern Trust delivers in both the Downe and Lagan Valley Hospital Urgent Care Centres, has not changed. In September 2023 the Department of Health adopted new Emergency Department definitions, to align Northern Ireland’s definitions with those used by NHS England. This was an administrative amendment which did not change or downgrade any services provided in the Urgent Care Centres. When the new definition was adopted, both the Downe and Lagan Valley Hospitals’ Urgent Care Centres were reclassified from a Type 2, to a Type 3.There are now no Type 2 facilities in Northern Ireland. (ii) No consultation is required for an administrative change in terminology.  The South Eastern HSC Trust is aware of the changes to the data recording practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust being the only Trust operating with just one Type 1 Emergency Department, which is located in Belfast.
answer_text: The South Eastern HSC Trust comprises one acute (area) hospital at the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald supported by local hospitals at Downpatrick, Lisburn, Bangor and Newtownards.  Emergency Departments are only safe and sustainable at acute hospitals.The Review of Public Administration (2002-2006) led to the abolition of 19 Trusts in Northern Ireland and the creation of 5 acute hospital and community Trusts in 2007 along with a regional ambulance service Trust.    The South Eastern Trust was established with an acute hospital, the Ulster Hospital, at Dundonald to cover the local government districts of Ards, North Down, Lisburn and Down.  Lagan Valley in Lisburn, Downe in Downpatrick, Bangor and Newtownards hospitals were identified as local supporting hospitals within the Trust’s area.  Although Lagan Valley and Downe Hospitals had Emergency Departments when the Trust was created, these were not sustainable and they now provide consultant led Urgent Care Centres. The consolidation of the Trust’s emergency care at Dundonald ensures that services remain safe and sustainable for the South Eastern population.  The provision of emergency care at the Downe Hospital is neither safe nor sustainable without 24 hour-7 days- a-week rapid diagnostics and full resuscitation facilities and associated services.  These are all provided at Dundonald.  Prior to the Trust’s decision to consolidate Emergency Care at the Ulster Hospital, it was unable to fill a number of long-term consultant vacancies which led to a reduction in opening hours initially and eventually the development of a clinically appropriate Urgent Care Centre.More recently, my Department publicly consulted on ‘Creating A Network for Better Outcomes’ which proposes a network of hospitals and the services to be provided in each locality.  This outlined the core services of Regional Centres, General Hospitals, Area Hospitals and Local Hospitals across the HSC.The outcome report of the consultation is expected later this year but proposes that the Ulster Hospital remains an Area Hospital for the South Eastern Trust.  The provision of emergency care at the other hospital sites across the Trust, is neither safe nor sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department has taken to reduce waiting times for families accessing the Education Authority’s special educational needs helpline.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the SEND Support Services digital phone system went live on 3 February 2025.   The SEND Central Helpline provides a live dashboard which is managed continuously each day by call queue managers who can allocate additional staff if the waiting times are excessive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of calls (i) the Education Authority special educational needs helpline has received since July 2025; and the average call duration; (ii) that went unanswered or were terminated;
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:the number of calls to the EA SEND Central helpline from 01 July 2025 to 12 September 2025 was 14,555. The average call duration was three minutes and 11 seconds. 8.2% of the calls were abandoned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to address the shortfall between Housing Executive grant allocations and the cost of construction.
answer_text: The maximum amount payable for a Disabled Facilities Grant increased from £50,000 to £70,000 in 2023.  This limit is set in legislation.  Any further increase would require a change in legislation.  Where appropriate, the Housing Executive signposts an applicant to potential bursary and other grant schemes, which may enable contributions towards the works to be completed if they are not fully met by the grant award.My officials are currently engaged with the Housing Executive and Department of Health as a part of the wider review of NIHE-administered Private Sector Grants, including consideration of whether a further increase to these upper limits is necessary, ways to make this a more flexible limit and whether any other forms of assistance can be made available in cases where the adaptation works exceed the £70,000 limit. I have prioritised the completion of the review within this mandate. I intend to bring forward policy proposals for consultation next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work her Department has undertaken with the Department for Health to review the Mental Health Order 1986, with a view to introducing a statutory duty of care on the PSNI to detain and remove a person to a place of safety for assessment, with a focus on strengthening preventative measures to reduce harm.
answer_text: The Mental Health Order 1986 (Northern Ireland) (The Order) provides the legal framework for the assessment, treatment and care of individuals with mental disorders, including provisions for their detention in specific circumstances. The Order empowers certain professionals, such as Approved Social Workers, and Medical Practitioners to initiate detention for assessment or treatment. It also provides powers for individuals to be removed, and detained, under certain circumstances, to a place of safety. My Department is actively working with an extensive range of key agencies, including the Department of Justice and the PSNI, to revise the Mental Health Order Code of Practice. This revision will offer increased guidance to stakeholders, including that in relation to Police Powers and Places of Safety. However there are no plans to introduce a statutory duty of care. The Code of Practice is considered best practice and although not legally binding, non-compliance may be referenced in legal proceedings.  The revised Code of Practice will be subject to a public consultation, which is being planned for launch in the near future. My officials also continue to engage with PSNI, and other relevant bodies, through a number of workstreams, to support and address issues in relation to the Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the specific actions that have been taken to enhance mental health services and support in South Down.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust provides a comprehensive range of mental health services and supports to residents in South Down. These include emergency, urgent and routine assessments through the Mental Health Assessment Centre, community mental health services with physical health monitoring, home treatment teams, and acute inpatient facilities including psychiatric intensive care, dementia and addictions units. Specialist provision is also available through psychological therapies, community addiction services, the Dialectical Behaviour Therapy programme, and the perinatal mental health team.In addition, the Trust delivers suicide prevention and postvention supports through the Towards Zero Suicide programme, bereavement services, and partnership working with the Public Health Agency and the community and voluntary sector. Recovery and peer support are prioritised through inpatient peer advocacy, the Service User Consultant role, Recovery College courses, the Wellness Recovery Network, and carers’ services. The Trust also funds day services, talking therapies, and counselling in the local community, and remains an active member of the South Eastern Protect Life Implementation Group.Through this combination of assessment, treatment, recovery, prevention and partnership initiatives, the Trust continues to enhance mental health services and strengthen support for the South Down community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which GP practices in South Down are currently working in a staffing deficit.
answer_text: General Practitioners (GPs) in Northern Ireland are independent contractors who are responsible for employing their own staff.  It is the responsibility of each GP practice to ensure that it is adequately staffed to provide safe and effective care to its registered patient list.I am therefore not able to detail which GP practices in South Down are currently working in a staffing deficit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to increase GP appointment capacity in South Down.
answer_text: I recognise the continuing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs.  My Department has taken forward a number of wide-ranging actions to help support GPs to adapt flexibly to increasing demand and to increase capacity within general practice.  The General Medical Services (GMS) contract for 2025/26 will see an additional investment of £9.5m into core GP services. This encompasses £5m to address the issue of GP indemnity; £3.5m to mitigate the cost of the increase in employer’s National Insurance costs, and a further £1m core investment. In addition to this, I recently announced investment of £61 million in expanding Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDTs) over the next four years. This is in addition to over £25m already invested in MDTs for primary care.This investment will see the MDT model brought to completion in the 6 GP Federations which have already commenced, including Newry District and Down GP Federations, and expansion into 5 new areas including Craigavon GP Federation, My Department also continues to focus on building the GP workforce and supporting recruitment and retention.  The increase in GP training places to 121 per year will be maintained, despite pressures in the training budget. This represents an increase of an increase of 86% from 65 places in 2015/16.  The GP Training Group, which includes the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency and GP representatives, will also progress work to project the number of GP training places required over the next 5 yearsThere are a number of schemes in place to support GPs, including the Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme, the GP Retainer scheme, the GP Mentor and Fellowship schemes, and the Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher scheme (now known as the International Induction Programme and the Return to Practice Programme).My Department is also taking forward work to develop a new model of neighbourhood care that will enable providers to work more effectively together, that delivers more care in primary and community settings in order to enable people to stay healthier for longer, reducing the demand for expensive, hospital-led care. This work will proceed at pace in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department will undertake over the summer to ensure that all outstanding school maintenance will be completed before September 2025.
answer_text: Significant work is being progressed in schools over the summer holiday period to address inescapable statutory requirements, to keep schools open and safe and to provide additional specialist places for children with Special Educational Needs.  		The schools’ estate is in need of increased and sustained funding to address the many years of underinvestment, and I will continue to make the case for this additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the new proposed funding model for the Pre-School Education Programme; (ii) will the new funding model aim to create funding parity among all early years providers; and (iii) when will the new funding model be made available.
answer_text: I approved a new funding model, which includes increased funding rates for non-statutory providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) on 10 June 2025.  The new model is intended to facilitate the approved session length for each setting, plus paid release time for activities such as planning, administration and training as well as overheads such as rent, utilities and consumables. In addition, an uplift has also been applied to echo the additional pupil-related factors that are included in funding to statutory pre-school settings; while the mechanism of payment on a real-time monthly basis has been retained to account for the particular needs of the non-statutory sector in regard to prompt payment.Under the new model the funding rates per-pupil in a funded non-statutory pre-school setting will be £4,316 for full-time provision and £2,805 for part-time provision, with both new rates effective from 1st September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department will undertake over the summer to ensure that essential infrastructure to meet the needs of children with physical disabilities will be completed before September 2025.
answer_text: All DDA pupil-specific and Special Educational Needs (SEN) related capital works are prioritised for delivery.  Officials in my SEN Capital Investment Programme Team work closely with colleagues in the Education Authority (EA) to track the progress of the projects and ensure where possible works will be completed before September 2025.There are a number of projects where works will not complete until after the start of the new term, and the EA is actively engaging with these schools to establish contingency arrangements for affected pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether he is aware that Knockevin Special School in South Down have made a request to the Education Authority to fill vacant special educational needs places, to ensure that there would be provision for early years children who currently do not have a school place for the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Statutory Assessment and Review Service were advised by the school principal that the school would want to offer a small number of places to children with Profound and Multiple Learning Disabilities (PMLD) SEN.  The number of children in the area with this profile of need is less than 5.  The Statutory Assessment and Review Service will, in line with parental preference, consult with schools in line with the assessment of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of Profound and Multiple Learning Disabilities places required for September 2025, in the South Down area.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that;There are 15 National Curriculum Year, <5 that have Profound and Multiple Learning Disabilities as a Primary Need, <5 of which is South Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why statutory and non-statutory nursery units were excluded from the Teacher Professional Learning Fund; and (ii) what plans he has to fund the professionalisation of the early years sector to support the ongoing development of early years providers.
answer_text: The new Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) Fund, which I launched on 23 June 2025 as a key part of the TransformED NI Strategy and Delivery Plan, is  a specific programme dedicated to supporting the professional development of teachers across all grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland. This fund is specifically designed to empower school leaders to implement professional learning programmes that are directly aligned with the identified learning needs of their teaching staff. As grant-aided schools, all statutory nursery schools and nursery units are fully included and funded under this programme. The Department places equal value on both statutory and non-statutory pre-school education provision. This principle is reflected in current policy and will be further embedded in the forthcoming Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, which is scheduled for publication this autumn. In support of non-statutory pre-school settings, I wrote directly to all providers on 10 June 2025 to outline a revised funding model for the pre-school education programme. This funding model is intended to support the approved session length for each setting, as well as provide paid release time for essential activities such as planning, administration, training and access to Early Years Specialist support.The Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will also propose actions related to workforce development and training. The level of funding allocated to these actions will be subject to consideration and approval by the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the potential social and economic benefit from hosting The 153rd Open at Royal Portrush.
answer_text: We are incredibly proud to be hosting the 153rd Open Championship at Royal Portrush this July. This is a huge moment for us — one that brings real benefits for our local economy and our tourism sector. Building on the estimated £100 million impact of the last Open in 2019, the 153rd Championship is predicted to generate more than £213 million in total economic benefit, according to the Sport Industry Research Centre (SIRC) at Sheffield Hallam University. The Open also puts us on the global stage, with estimates for TV coverage reaching over 100 million homes and attracting almost 280,000 attendees to the event —showing the world everything we have to offer. Local businesses, jobs and communities right across the region will feel the benefit. It is of course more than just a sporting event — it inspires pride, brings people together, and leaves a lasting legacy. We are pleased to have supported The Tourism NI “Epic Journey,” which has engaged families, inspired school children and given local sports fans the opportunity to see the prestigious Claret Jug in the build up to The Championship. To mark this milestone, we hosted a special event on Tuesday 27 May, celebrating the collective effort that brought The Championship back to Portrush. The Open is highlighting Northern Ireland for all that it has to offer as a place to live, work, visit and invest. We look forward to welcoming the world this summer

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the current number of vacant properties in South Down; and (i) how many of these are under the control or ownership of the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the requested information for Registered Housing Associations or private properties.    (ii)      This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(ii) As of 23rd May 2025, there were 24 actionable* and 68 non-actionable** void Housing Executive properties in the South Down Area Office.*Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated and include properties undergoing change of tenancy repairs and difficult to let properties.**Non-Actionable voids are properties that are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons – for example, decanting purposes (where they are being used to house a tenant on a temporary basis pending works to their home), planned maintenance schemes and properties which have been identified for sale/transfer or have approval for demolition.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what proposals did he submit to the British government to ensure the fisheries sector will not be disadvantaged due to reduced access on the island of Ireland.
answer_text: Under the Trade and Cooperation Agreement, the UK and EU are in an ‘adjustment period’ until June 2026, during which reciprocal access to each other’s waters is guaranteed. After this, access would have been negotiated annually with a presumption that EU vessels would largely continue to access UK waters and vice versa. The extension of the fishing agreement means that the fishing opportunities available to the EU in UK waters and vice versa will be maintained until June 2038. The agreement includes access to each other’s Exclusive Economic Zones and in specified areas to waters between six and twelve nautical miles.  The fisheries sector and my Department have been highlighting since the Trade and Cooperation Agreement was first agreed in December 2020 that it should have provided for Northern Ireland vessels to access the Republic of Ireland six to twelve nautical mile zone.In anticipation of the UK EU Summit my officials represented the views of the Northern Ireland fishing industry to the UK Government and sought access to the Republic of Ireland’s six to twelve nautical mile zone.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work he has undertaken with the Department of Communities to ensure that childcare is made more accessible by reducing the shortfall between the 85% of childcare costs which are paid back to families on Universal Credit and the full cost they pay.
answer_text: The childcare element of Universal Credit (UC), including the percentage contribution provided, is a matter for the Department for Communities (DfC).  DfC advises that the reimbursement of up to 85% of the cost of childcare for eligible Universal Credit claimants is a policy maintained by the Department for Work and Pensions in Great Britain. The same policy is implemented in Northern Ireland as a parity measure.  Diverging from parity on social security issues has financial implications for Northern Ireland as the costs of any such divergence have to be met by the Northern Ireland Executive.  On 28 June 2023, DfC, in parity with DWP, increased the maximum payable amount of the UC childcare element from £646.35 to £950.92 for one child and from £1,108.04 to £1,630.15 for two or more children. These amounts were again increased in April 2025 to £1,031.88 and £1,768.94 respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has had with the Minister for Agriculture, Food, Fisheries and the Marine on realising the all-island potential of collaborating on fisheries and aquaculture.
answer_text: Whilst I have not had any engagement with my Irish counterpart on this particular issue, my officials engage and collaborate regularly with officials in the Department of Agriculture Food and the Marine other Irish Government Departments on matters relating to fisheries, aquaculture and the marine environment. This includes management of the all-Ireland seed mussel fishery, matters relating to aquatic animal health, management of inland fish stocks, marine planning and marine environment policy. In addition, I will continue to engage with my counterpart Irish Ministers in my role as Minister on the North-South Ministerial Council sectoral meetings on Environment, Agriculture and Aquaculture & Marine and welcome all future engagement and collaborative working which supports sustainable development of the fisheries and aquaculture sectors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the £360 million fund for fishing and coastal communities, what bids has his Department submitted to support (i) the fisheries sector in South Down; (ii) the development of Ardglass Harbour; and (iii) the development of Kilkeel Harbour.
answer_text: The proposed £360 million Fishing and Coastal Communities Fund is under development and not yet open for bids or applications. UK Government intends to engage with industry stakeholders to help inform and shape the Fund, which is unlikely to open for applications until 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's work to reduce restrictions on the fishing sector accessing Seasonal Worker visas.
answer_text: I recognise that Northern Ireland’s fishing sector has faced several challenges in recent years, one of which has been to secure labour. The circumstances are intensified by some of the proposals contained within the UK Government’s Immigration White Paper. The changes in the White Paper have not taken any account of the specific needs of the agri-food and fishing sectors, or Northern Ireland’s unique circumstances. The increase in the overall skills threshold for migration to RQF 6 (undergraduate level) effectively removes eligibility of fishing occupations from the Skilled Worker Route. It is crucial that these roles are included on the new Temporary Shortage List, with a salary that is appropriate and in keeping with the going rate for these occupations.I have recently communicated these issues in detail with the Defra Secretary of State, Home Secretary and Minister for Migration and Citizenship. I have provided feedback on the UK Government’s Immigration White Paper following its publication on 12th May 2025 and reiterated immigration policy proposals which would provide solutions to these challenges, including those put forward by the NI Fishermen’s Federation (NIFF). The First Minister and deputy First Minister have also communicated these issues to the Minister for Migration and Citizenship.DAERA officials regularly attend meetings of the Defra Access to Labour Devolved Governments group and have raised the topic of shared learnings for the fishing industry with regards visa support. Defra is currently undertaking a UK Seafood Careers Project (UKSfCP), which aims to explore issues and challenges affecting recruitment and retention of domestic labour within the UK's fishing, processing, and aquaculture sectors. My Department is working with the Department for the Economy in relation to making further representations to the Home Secretary on issues arising from the White Paper.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the process for non-statutory early years providers seeking to obtain statutory status.
answer_text: Funded pre-school provision in Northern Ireland is delivered via a partnership of statutory (nursery schools and nursery units) and non-statutory (private and voluntary) settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP).  All providers are equally valued, deliver to the same curricular guidelines, and are inspected to the same educational standards by the education and training Inspectorate.  For any significant change to a school, such as the establishment of a statutory nursery school or unit, legislation currently requires the publication and approval of a statutory Development Proposal (DP). The DE website provides information regarding the DP process at DP Guidance October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a list of specific 100 pre-school settings currently under consideration for inclusion in the 2025–26 Pre-School Education Programme.
answer_text: Information on the pre-schools transitioning to full-time provision from September 2025 is published by constituency on the Department website at Minister’s Decisions on Pre-School Settings Requesting Transition from September 2025 | Department of Education.  The pre-school settings which will transition in September 2026 have yet to be identified. All settings had the opportunity to provide updated information regarding their self-assessed state of readiness to transition to full-time provision in a survey conducted by the EA earlier this year.  This information will be used to inform further engagement between settings and the EA over the coming months.It is the intention to begin consultation during Autumn 2025 on the individual settings being considered for approval to transition from September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) will new SureStart provision be established in Super Output Areas that currently do not have SureStart provision; and (ii) to outline details of proposed SureStart area expansion.
answer_text: Sure Start services provided by the existing 38 Sure Start projects will be made available to additional children and families who live beyond the current Sure Start geographical boundaries and who require Sure Start support.  Rather than applying a geographical approach by incorporating whole Super Output Areas, a targeted outreach model will be implemented ensuring that children with demonstrable need can be identified and referred into the programme, regardless of where they live.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to define the age range of school-aged children who will be included in the extension of the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: The age range of school-aged children who will be included in the extension of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme aligns with the eligibility criteria for Tax Free Childcare (TFC), which is set by His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs.TFC eligibility states that a child must be 11 or under, they stop being eligible on 1 September after their 11th birthday. Children with disabilities are eligible until 1 September after their 16th birthday.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how many additional SureStart places will be created through the 2025-26 Early Learning and Childcare funding (ELC) ; (ii) how many additional staff positions will be created through the 2025-26 ELC funding; and (iii) a breakdown of the geographical spread of these additional places and jobs, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: (i) 	2025-26 Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) funding will enable up to 1,810 additional children and their families to avail of Sure Start support, through the introduction of a new outreach model. (ii) and (iii) Implementation plans for the outreach model are being developed by the Department of Health which administers the Sure Start programme on behalf of the Department of Education. At this stage it is not possible to determine what additional staffing will be required, if any, to deliver this expansion of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on efforts to introduce additional flexibility to the procurement process for schools.
answer_text: The revision of Procurement Policy Note (PPN) 04/21 in March 2025 increased the thresholds which determine the procurement processes for lower value purchases which contracting authorities, including schools, must follow. The threshold at which a contracting authority must evidence two price checks has increased from £5,000 to £10,000.  Given this update to PPN 04/21, I have instructed the Education Authority (EA) to increase the schools’ procurement control limit to £10,000 for purchases which require two evidenced price checks.  This change will provide schools with more flexibility for those procurements below £10,000 and which are not already available through an existing framework.  This change has been communicated to schools via numerous EA channels including C2K and the EA Procurement portal.In addition to this change, I have instructed my officials to engage with the EA to explore further options available specifically in relation to schools’ use of frameworks.  The EA is exploring options for providing schools with greater flexibility in this area of spend, including an assessment of the potential benefits, risks and impact of any changes which may be considered or piloted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered the introduction of a Preventable Deaths Tracker to address the ongoing impact of suicide and all preventable deaths.
answer_text: The Department of Health is not currently considering the introduction of a preventable deaths tracker as is used in England and Wales. This issue would require consideration by the Coroners Service as it relates to data arising from coroners’ reports.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has considered the introduction of a Preventable Deaths Tracker to address the ongoing impact of preventable deaths.
answer_text: I am actively considering the matter of future preventable deaths. The Preventable Deaths Tracker, currently operating in England and Wales, is based on coronial death reports to prevent future deaths, which are published by the Chief Coroner under coronial legislation which does not currently apply in Northern Ireland. This matter will form part of my broader considerations on the potential modernisation and reform of Northern Ireland's coronial legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of temporary flexibilities have been given to statutory nursery schools in the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: 109 pre-school places were approved via the Temporary Flexibility process for statutory nursery schools and nursery units during the 2024/25 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that children who attend voluntary, community and independent pre-schools are not disadvantaged from the Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund due to the fact that they cannot refer children to educational psychologists.
answer_text: Children in voluntary, community and independent pre-school settings can access the Education Authority’s (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) through parental or healthcare professional referral.The breakdown of pre-school education settings who received funding in 2024/25 under the pilot Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) was 61.5% statutory nursery settings and 38.5% non-statutory pre-school settings.  The number of non-statutory settings who received funding was proportionally slightly higher than the overall breakdown of pre-school provision, which in 2024/25 is 69.5% statutory provision and 30.5% non-statutory provision.  There has, therefore, been no disadvantage to children in non-statutory settings in the allocation of this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether Kids Together funding will be extended for 2025-26; and (ii) whether additional funding will be allocated for 2025-26.
answer_text: Both Sólás and Kids Together are two of the four delivery partners of the Belfast Wide Early Years (BWEY) pilot.  I have approved the continuation of funding for this pilot to the end of this academic year (30 June 2025) and partners have been advised.My officials are currently reviewing the independent evaluation of the pilot and any decisions on additional funding will be taken in consideration of that evaluation and in the context of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether Sólás funding will be extended for 2025-26; and (ii) whether additional funding will be allocated for 2025-26.
answer_text: Both Sólás and Kids Together are two of the four delivery partners of the Belfast Wide Early Years (BWEY) pilot.  I have approved the continuation of funding for this pilot to the end of this academic year (30 June 2025) and partners have been advised.My officials are currently reviewing the independent evaluation of the pilot and any decisions on additional funding will be taken in consideration of that evaluation and in the context of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the Education Authority advisory group that determines Published Admission Number is working to ensure all children are placed in a pre-school setting in their locality.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in cooperation with the EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG), is responsible for setting Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory pre-school settings.  The EA wrote to providers on 7 February to set out the process agreed by the EA PEG for the allocation of initial PANs, as well as the criteria for reviewing those allocations. This involves a continuing assessment of supply and demand in local areas, to determine where PAN adjustments are required to meet identified need.  The EA PEG provides advice throughout the admissions process which is still ongoing.   While the EA PEG works to ensure sufficient provision in local areas, admissions criteria are set by pre-school providers themselves and do not necessarily prioritise local families.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to include a Pathway Fund award stream for Childminders and Approved Home Childcarers, including any information on a maximum pathway award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of special educational needs children that have unallocated social workers, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) constituency.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of special educational needs adults post-19 that have unallocated social workers, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) constituency.
answer_text: Children who have special educational needs (SEN), may remain in education until the age of 19. At 18 years they are considered to be adults, in keeping with salient legislation and policy. In the main Social Workers within Children’s services remain operationally responsible for young people with Learning Disabilities until the age of 18. Adult Learning Disability Teams become involved in advance of the young person’s 18th birthday, to support the young person to transition into adult services. Therefore, it is likely that any adult between the age of 18 and 19 within a SEN school, has an allocated social worker. Adults with Learning Disabilities beyond the age of 19 are not commonly referred to as having special educational needs, as most people at this age will have transitioned out of SEN schools. It is important to also recognise that people are referred to adult Learning Disability Services for Social Work intervention from community sources, other than education, from the age of 18.The number of Adults with Learning Disabilities awaiting allocation of a Social Worker, as reported by Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) is as follows;Health and Social Care TrustNorthern Health and Social Care Trust0Southern Health and Social Care Trust155Western Health and Social Care Trust64South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust27Belfast Health and Social Care Trust0Variance in the data provided by HSCTs is attributed to differences in recognised terminology. Specifically, adults with special educational needs, limits the data range to those aged 18-19 years. Reporting of adults with special educational needs post-19 will vary depending on operational application of terminology and referral source. Not all adults with Learning Disabilities with unallocated social workers will have been referred from education.My Department is not in a position to breakdown figures on the basis of Parliamentary or NI Assembly constituency boundaries, as these are no longer aligned to Health and Social Care Trust boundaries, in which data is collated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline for when (i) a full-time multiple sclerosis (MS) nurse will be reinstated in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the MS helpline be back operational.
answer_text: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Nurses provide specialist nursing skills and knowledge in the care and management of those affected by Multiple Sclerosis, their families and the professionals working with them. As part of our Regional Commissioning arrangements, there are dedicated MS nursing posts in all five Trusts.The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) is working to ensure that nursing support to the wider MS team is available as soon as possible and is actively recruiting for a full-time band 7 MS Nurse, with appointment expected shortly following an interview process that is currently underway.Whilst there have been some operational challenges, the Multiple Sclerosis helpline has always remained operational for blood monitoring queries and appointment queries. Once appointed, the MS Nurse will provide additional support to the wider MS team.SEHSCT management engaged with the MS Society on 1 April 2025 to support communication to patient groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how frequently the Education Authority writes to early years providers to discuss Published Admission Number (PAN) allocation; (ii) whether correspondence differed between the statutory and non-statutory sectors; and (iii) , once PAN is allocated, whether there is any additional correspondence accepted on behalf of providers to the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) engages with non-statutory pre-school providers on an annual basis in respect of PAN allocations and with individual settings throughout the course of the pre-school admissions process and beyond, on specific issues, including PAN adjustments where these are required. A copy of the EA letter that issued to all non-statutory providers setting out the arrangements for the 2025-26 academic year has been placed in the Assembly library.  Settings can also submit correspondence to the EA in respect of their PAN allocation at any time. Requests for a change to PAN allocations will be considered by the EA in line with the criteria set out in the EA letter.Statutory pre-school settings are not allocated a PAN, but rather they are set an admissions and enrolment number via a legislative process as set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.  The EA does not therefore routinely correspond with statutory pre-school providers in respect of PAN allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will extend approved admission numbers to non-statutory early year providers to match statutory early years settings.
answer_text: The EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for securing non-statutory pre-school education provision in local areas and for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available in all areas to accommodate every target aged child whose parents want it.  In each of the past 6 years, at least 99.8% of children have received the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference.The EA will continue to allocate funded pre-school places based on an assessment of overall need in local areas.  This approach allows the number of places available each year to increase or decrease in response to changing birth rates, thereby minimising overprovision and unnecessary costs.  It is also essential to ensuring the stability of the PSEP and the pre-school sector overall.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to extend the common funding scheme to non-statutory early years providers to ensure that children from disadvantaged backgrounds, newcomers, Travellers and children in care receive the same funding as if they were in a statutory preschool.
answer_text: All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. As set out in the CFS, schools are funded based on the enrolment data on the school census immediately preceding the financial year to which funding relates (i.e. 2024-25 CFF is based upon the October 2023 census data).  Non-statutory pre-school providers have advised that they require a different type of funding arrangement as it would be financially unsustainable for them to receive funding retrospectively based on census enrolment data.  The current approach ensures that they receive funding on a monthly basis, which reflects the number of pupils they have enrolled for that period (i.e. 2024-25 funding is based on 2024-25 attendance), an arrangement that is not possible within the CFF. I have already approved a significant uplift to the non-statutory pre-school sector funding rate, taking into account the particular characteristics and requirements of providers and the needs of children attending those settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will use the additional £50 million allocated for childcare to introduce any cost cutting measures for parents ahead of the expected increased cost of childcare by the end of March 2025.
answer_text: I am reviewing the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) parameters to ensure that it remains effective in reducing childcare costs for working families. Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues for approval.  Only at that point will the £50m be available to my department.I have already announced that the NICSS will continue into 2025/26 and the subsidy cap, which is the maximum monthly amount that can be claimed per child, will be increased by 10% to reflect the anticipated rise in childcare fees from April 2025.  I have also raised the administrative payment paid to providers delivering the scheme to ensure that any additional operating costs associated with its implementation are not passed on to parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding, per child, is allocated to (i) non-statutory part-time preschool places; (ii) statutory part-time preschool places; (iii) non-statutory full-time preschool places; and (iv) statutory full-time preschool places.
answer_text: The Common Funding Formula is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to Statutory pre-school settings funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements.  The current pupil Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) for statutory pre-school settings is set out below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the criteria used to determine how Pupil Allocation Number is allocated; and (ii) whether the criteria differs between statutory and non-statutory settings.
answer_text: Pupil Allocation Numbers (PAN) are only applicable to non-statutory pre-school settings.The EA has written to all non-statutory providers setting out in detail the criteria for the allocation of PANs and the review process.  A copy of this letter has been placed in the Assembly library. Statutory pre-school settings are not allocated a PAN, but rather they are set an admissions and enrolment number via a legislative process as set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.  Unlike non-statutory settings, statutory settings cannot change the number of places offered to reflect either an increase or decrease in demand without following the relevant legislative processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12; (ii) for a delivery date of when the review will be implemented; and (iii) whether he will he ensure child to staff ratios for non-statutory and statutory early years sectors will be equal, in line with the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: The first stage of the review of the Minimum Standards, a public consultation on adult-child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP, was launched on Monday 24 March 2025.Following the close of the consultation on 20 June 2025, all responses will be collated for review by the Department, and a consultation report will be produced. If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers. While my Department is responsible for setting the ratios within childcare, the ratios within statutory pre-school education provision is a matter for the Education Minister.  Following the completion of the wider review, I will consult publicly on a new set of Standards prior to publication.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) percentage; and (ii) number of children that were offered a funded place in a preschool setting of their parents’ first preference in the last six years.
answer_text: The aim of the DE Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) is to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it.  It is not the intention of the programme, nor is it possible, to provide a funded place for every child in their first preference setting. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether the Pre-School Standardisation Programme has (i) created an unintentional unfairness among preschools that have not yet been standardised; and (ii) created an unintentional unfairness within standardised settings who have been offered less standardised places than their capacity.
answer_text: Pupils Admission Numbers (PAN) are an operational matter for the EA and the majority of pre-school settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) have received the same PAN for 2025/26 as their actual enrolment number in October 2024.While settings due to transition to a full-time session length from September 2025 have seen an increase in first preferences, the EA has made it clear that in the allocation of PANs for 2025/26, these settings will not be given preferential treatment above those which have yet to transition.  In practice, this means that while the EA may increase PANs in some funded pre-school settings to meet an overall shortfall of provision in a local area, it will do so irrespective of the session length offered by the setting.  This ensures that all settings are treated fairly during the implementation phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) a breakdown of the £51 million special educational needs (SEN) budget; (ii) the total budget allocation for Strand 1 of the SEN budget targeting maintenance and minor works; (iii) the total budget allocation for Strand 2 for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools; (iv) the total budget allocation for Strand 3 for additional provision for special schools; and (v) the total budget allocation for Strand 4 for additional special schools.
answer_text: The total SEN budget for 2024/25 is currently £50.443m.  The SEN capital investment programme has four strands:Strand 1 - annual maintenance and equipment programme Strand 2 - new specialist provisions in mainstream Strand 3 - extension and refurbishment of special schoolsStrand 4 - new special schools £42.243m has been allocated to the EA to deliver the SEN capital investment programme in special schools and controlled mainstream schools across the four strands.  £8.2m was allocated to the SEN capital investment team in my department for specialist provision and associated works in non-controlled schools (Strand 2) as my department has budget responsibility for capital works in these schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his plan to address the gap between those pre-school providers who have been successful in the roll-out of the Pre-School Standardisation programme and those who have not; and (ii) any engagement he has had with those who feel they are at a disadvantage.
answer_text: The Pre-school Standardisation Programme is moving at considerable pace, but it will take a number of years before every setting place is full time. Full-time and part-time provision operating side by side has been a feature of our system for many years. This is not a new situation created by standardisation.  However, I appreciate that some settings may have concerns about being treated differently to those that have made the transition before them.  I have received correspondence from both settings that are transitioning who are naturally keen to secure as many places as possible, as well as settings that are part-time, who have concerns that they could suffer if full-time providers are prioritised by the EA.  It is essential at this time of transition that all are treated fairly.The EA, which is responsible for both the pre-school admissions process and the pre-school standardisation programme is taking pro-active steps to ensure that all funded pre-school settings are supported and treated equitably.  The EA will continue to allocate funded pre-school education places based on an assessment of need in local areas and settings that transition to the standardised session length will not be given preference above those yet to transition in the process.Since my appointment as Minister of Education I have engaged regularly with representatives of the pre-school sector on many issues, including the Pre-School Standardisation Programme, through correspondence, meetings and visits to listen to their views and see first-hand the wonderful services they provide for our children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the boundaries, by postcode, of the (i) Southern Health and Social Care Trust area; and (ii) South Eastern Health and Social Trust area, within the South Down constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data in the format requested, by individual constituency area, as the Trust areas are not defined in legislation by postcode but by local government district.The Southern Trust area is defined as the local government districts of Armagh, Banbridge, Craigavon, Dungannon and Newry and Mourne.  The South Eastern Trust area is defined as the local government districts of Ards, Down, Lisburn and North Down.Information defining all postcodes falling within Trust areas is available to my Department, as held within the Central Postcode Directory, which is compiled and maintained for users by the NISRA Geography Department within the Census office.  This Central Postcode Directory contains an indirect look up table, which enables users to attribute postcodes to individual Health Trust areas.  There are multiple thousands of individual postcodes falling within the requested Health Trust areas.  On that basis it is not practical to list within an Assembly Question response.It may be that the Census office within NIRSA may be able to provide you with further advice and guidance on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the current provision to reduce the number of standardised places in a locality if one preschool setting is under subscribed will be rectified to ensure the highest number of children avail of the Pre-School Education Programme.
answer_text: In each of the past 6 years, at least 99.8% of children have received the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference.The EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for securing non-statutory pre-school education provision in local areas and for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available in all areas to accommodate every target aged child whose parents want it.  The EA is also responsible for leading on the Pre-School Standardisation Programme (PSP) and must ensure that all funded pre-school settings are treated equitably throughout the period of transition.  The EA will continue to allocate funded pre-school places based on an assessment of overall need in local areas and settings that transition to the standardised session length will not be given preference above those yet to transition in the process.  This approach is essential to ensuring the stability of the PSEP and the pre-school sector overall.  In the context of a falling birth rate across Northern Ireland, increasing places in one setting would invariably mean reducing them for another, potentially undermining the viability of the latter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will create a system where childcare staff are able to make referrals directly to educational psychologists in order to ensure early intervention practices are utilised to support the development of children.
answer_text: Currently referrals to the Education Authority’s (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) are accepted from schools or by a health professional for children of pre-school age and not attending a nursery unit or school.Parents may also make a request for consideration of statutory assessment of their child’s educational needs.There are currently no plans to introduce a system whereby childcare staff can make referrals to educational psychology.My Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan will build a coherent and targeted approach to Early Intervention.  This will include a range of actions to ensure children can access intervention at the earliest point when needs emerge.The Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy is being developed and will consider actions to better assist providers in supporting children with emerging additional needs.

answer_text: In each of the past 6 years, at least 99.8% of children have received the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference.The EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for securing non-statutory pre-school education provision in local areas and for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available in all areas to accommodate every target aged child whose parents want it.
