question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of two year olds that have been placed in a nursery school under the Pre-School Education Programme; (ii) how much this has cost his Department; and (iii) the comparative cost if these two year olds were placed in a childcare setting.
answer_text: In the current 2024/25 academic year 1,396 penultimate age children have obtained a funded pre-school education place in a statutory pre-school setting.  A breakdown of the pupils is detailed in the table below: Setting TypeNumber of PenultimateNursery School (Full /Time)263Nursery School (Part/Time)325Nursery Unit (Full/Time)314Nursery Unit (Part/Time)494(ii) All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  Pupils that attend a nursery school or unit attract the same level of funding depending on the hours of attendance (part time/ full-time), irrespective of age.As set out in the CFS, schools are funded based on the enrolment data on the school census immediately preceding the financial year to which funding relates (i.e. 2024-25 CFF is based upon the October 2023 census data).  It is not possible to disaggregate the funding attributable to individual pupils from the total funding allocated to individual schools.  The table below provides details of the 2024-25 CFF Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) funding for nursery schools and units, for illustrative purposes. Setting TypeAWPU Rate (£)Nursery School (Full Time)4,093Nursery School (Part Time)2,692Nursery Unit (Full Time)3,806Nursery Unit (Part Time)2,523The cost attributable to penultimate age pupils attending nursery settings has to be considered in the context of the financial sustainability of these schools which will incur the staffing and running costs irrespective of pupil enrolments.The Department of Education does not have a role in regulating childcare fees for a sector which is largely made up of private providers who are responsible for setting their own childcare rates.As a result it is not possible to provide a comparative cost if these children attended a childcare setting instead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he intends to reduce cataract services in the Downe Hospital; and (ii) whether his Department consulted ophthalmology services staff in the Downe Hospital to ensure they can provide the highest quality of care.
answer_text: (i)	Prototype Cataract Day Procedure Centres (DPCs) were introduced in 2019/20 to act as a resource for the region, working across Trust boundaries to deliver the majority of non-complex cataract procedures, equalising waits and reducing both waiting times and waiting lists.Surgical waiting lists have reduced considerably, from over 3,700 people waiting more than one year on regional surgical cataract lists in March 2021, to just over 180 today.Downe hospital has played a significant role in delivering against this objective, performing in excess of 12,000 cataract procedures since December 2019, including support from in-reach independent providers.To date the prototype models have been funded non-recurrently, with Downe Hospital being in a position to offer up to 10 theatre sessions per week over a 42-week year. Prior to setting up the DPC, funding baseline was three sessions per week at Downe Hospital.Ongoing analysis of activity, performance, workforce availability, training need and prioritised non-cataract, including time-critical, demand concludes that seven cataract theatre sessions per week should be commissioned for cataract, an increase on the three sessions per week baseline.In addition, my officials are currently exploring adding additional non-cataract (glaucoma and potentially minor-op) activity to complement the cataract activity and develop the facility as an ophthalmology centre of excellence.(ii) I can confirm that, through the Clinical Director for Ophthalmology, via Elective Care Management structures, and with SET senior management team, regular engagement has taken place throughout the planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the funding for the Belfast Wider Early Years group.
answer_text: I have agreed to continue funding for the Belfast Wide Early Years (BWEY) pilot to the end of this academic year.Any decisions on future funding will be taken in due course and in consideration of the independent evaluation of the pilot which was received by the department on 24 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his plans to make high-quality and accessible childcare more affordable.
answer_text: I am aware of price increases facing parents accessing childcare.  My Department does not have a role in regulating childcare fees for a sector which is largely made up of private providers, however, I am currently reviewing the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) parameters to ensure that it remains effective in reducing childcare costs. I have secured £50m for Early Learning and Childcare provision in the draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation. I am considering options for its allocation, based on what is affordable. When the budget is agreed, I will bring forward proposals to the Executive.I intend to bring forward an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered lifting the cap on the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: The administrative data generated from the operation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is being monitored, including the impact of the current cap.  All options to ensure that the subsidy remains effective in reducing the costs of childcare for parents are being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Chancellor in relation to lifting the cap for childcare tax credits.
answer_text: I had written to the Chancellor of the Exchequer in September 2024 to ask if she would consider increasing the £100k upper threshold for Tax-Free Childcare (TFC), reflecting the increase in salaries since 2017 when the Scheme was first introduced. A response from His Majesty’s Treasury (HMT) advised that the aim of TFC is to encourage parents to both return to work and also to increase their hours.  HMT’s position is that the £100,000 per parent cap on TFC ensures support to cover childcare costs is directed to those who most need it to be able to work and increasing the cap would reduce the amount of support that could be given to those on lower incomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to increase the ratios for registered childminders through the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12.
answer_text: The review commenced on Monday 24 March with the launch of a public consultation exercise seeking a wide range of views in relation to adult:child ratios and the health declarations which prospective childcare providers are required to produce prior to taking up work. I would encourage as many people as possible to respond to the consultation which is available on the Department’s website and Citizen Space.While all of the responses to that consultation exercise will inform my final decisions, my overall priority is to ensure that childcare remains a safe, high-quality experience for the children and families who use it.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust will be able to roll-out Givinostat through the Early Access Programme; and (ii) any work that he and the Chief Executive of the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have carried out to ensure patients who qualify for Givinostat for urgent medical intervention have access to it.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) is currently evaluating givinostat (Duvyzat®) for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in people of 6 years and over, with final guidance expected to publish in July 2025.  In Northern Ireland, the paediatric neuromuscular service is delivered by the Belfast Heath and Social Care Trust who have responsibility for participation in the Early Access Programmes for givinostat.  Whilst the manufacturer has made givinostat available via an Early Access Programme (EAP) for eligible patients in parts of the UK, the Belfast Trust has advised that it is unfortunately not in a position to facilitate the EAP due to the associated resource implications.The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust will however monitor the outcomes of the programme, and any potential roll out to the adult population. When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in line with the literacy support review and curriculum review, whether he has (i) considered that the evidence in Phonic screening checks and Key Stage 1 assessments in England which demonstrates that systematic synthetic phonics fails up to 25 per cent of children; and (ii) considered implementing a holistic approach driven by a systematic approach.
answer_text: Work is ongoing between my officials and Health colleagues to develop the operational detail of joint Health/Education initiatives within the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the Childcare Subsidy Scheme will be extended beyond March 2025; (ii) when childcare providers will be notified of this decision: (iii) the projected cost of the extension of the scheme; and (iv) whether support will be extended to school age children.
answer_text: Within the draft Budget which is out for public consultation, I have been able to secure the funding necessary to continue the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme into the next financial year.  The funding to do so will be confirmed when we agree it as an Executive.  I will formally notify providers of the arrangements for April 2025 as soon as possible.I am considering options for the year ahead and associated costs. I will bring recommendations to the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of times the Early Learning Childcare (ELC) Task and Finish Group have met; (ii) the stakeholders that make up the ELC Task and Finish Group; and (iii) the number of times he has met with the ELC Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: The Early Learning Childcare (ELC) Task and Finish Group has met in plenary format on five occasions, supplemented by sub-group and bi-lateral meetings on specific issues.   In addition to my department, the membership of the ELC Task and Finish Group includes the Department for the Economy, the Department for Communities, the Department of Health, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, the Department of Finance and the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA).It is a group of senior officials, and I do not attend the meetings.  My officials keep me informed of progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the childcare strategy will include the expansion of the Childcare Subsidy Scheme (CSS); (ii) whether the CSS will focus on school aged children or children under 4 years old; (iii) whether the CSS will include a cap; and (iv) any research his Department has undertaken to scope the feasibility of a cap.
answer_text: I have secured £50m for Early Learning and Childcare provision in the draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation.  This will allow the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) to continue into the next financial year.  I am considering options, based on what is affordable within this budget allocation, to ensure the scheme remains effective in supporting working parents with their childcare costs.  This involves reviewing the scheme parameters based on evidence collected to date, including information relating to the impact of the cap.    When the budget is agreed, I will bring forward proposals to the Executive.  Further development of the NICSS is being considered within the context of the ELC Strategy, consistent with the need to ensure provider sustainability and alignment with other financial support schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when the childcare strategy will be delivered; and (ii) whether providers have been consulted to ensure there is sufficient capacity for any increased measures.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward an Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.  This work is being supported by the cross departmental ELC Task and Finish Group and a wide range of stakeholders, including the Early Learning and Childcare Stakeholder Engagement Forum.  The capacity of the sector to deliver actions within the strategy will be a key consideration in determining a realistic implementation plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on funding for public sector transformation.
answer_text: Following advice from the interim Public Sector Transformation Board, the former Finance Minister tabled her recommendations on the transformation proposals to be funded. These were discussed at the Executive meeting on 23 January 2025.The Executive subsequently agreed Minister Archibald’s proposals on 28 January.While significant progress has been made, there remain a number of steps to be finalised.Minister Archibald met with the Secretary of State on 28 January to discuss the remaining issues, and then separately wrote to both he and the Chief Secretary on these matters.										Once these issues have been resolved, I will then be in a position to announce the proposals that have been successful.Having come from the Department for Infrastructure, I can say that the process undertaken has been a positive one. Officials have reflected to me that this process has challenged them, led to the development of stronger proposals, and allowed departments to demonstrate how they could accelerate the transformation of public services if funding was available.While we know that the £235m in transformation funding, over five years, will in no way tackle the magnitude of issues at hand, it can be significant in helping to develop and implement a model of delivery, to stimulate the wider transformation of public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Pre-School Education Programme standardisation programme.

To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) work carried out with the Department of Health on a strategy to end vaping on school grounds; (ii) recommendations by the Department of Health on resources and training to be available to teachers and school staff to respond to vaping in schools; and (iii) a plan to facilitate resources and training to aide teachers and school staff to reduce the number of young people vaping, particularly on school grounds.
answer_text: The issue of vaping is primarily a public health, and in certain circumstances, a criminal justice matter.  As such, we will follow appropriate guidance from the Department of Health / Public Health Agency (PHA) and Department of Justice/PSNI on this issue.  	In terms of resources to aid teachers in the delivery of lessons to pupils on the harmful risks associated with vaping, schools in Northern Ireland have the autonomy to decide which resources they use to support delivery of the curriculum. 		To further enhance curriculum delivery, schools are also free to engage with external organisations if they so wish.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support he will provide to grassroots sports clubs.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the research for a childcare business support scheme.
answer_text: Question 13 – AQO 1522/22-27The research to determine the financial health and needs of the childcare sector is progressing at pace.  Consultations have taken place with a cross-section of stakeholder organisations and representative organisations within the Childcare and wider sectors.  More detailed consultations with individual childcare providers and parents are taking place at present. The research remains on schedule to complete by March 2025. The resultant evidence base will allow my Department to move quickly to make recommendations to the Minister of Education on the nature of assistance required by Childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) progress made on the Tobacco and Vapes Bill; and (ii) his Department's work with the Department of Education on a strategy to end vaping on school grounds.
answer_text: The Tobacco and Vapes Bill is, at the time of writing, in the final stages of Parliament and expected to pass into law in the coming weeks. The Bill will provide powers to each UK administration to designate specific outdoor public places as smoke-free, vape-free and heated tobacco-free – subject to public consultation and engagement with key stakeholders.There are currently no plans to develop a strategy with the Department of Education (DE) to end vaping on school grounds. As part of the statutory curriculum, DE has a duty to deliver drugs education (including legal and illegal substances) in both primary and secondary schools. This should raise awareness amongst children of the risks and dangers associated with vaping. In addition, all schools have a statutory responsibility within their scheme of management (Articles 3-6 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998) for developing their own policies to promote good behaviour and discipline.  This could include policies to ensure that the premises for which they are responsible are both smoke and vape-free environments. A DE circular was issued in December 2014 which encouraged schools to extend the ban on smoking on school and youth premises to include a ban on the use of e-cigarettes. A further circular on Drugs Guidance which states that 'e-cigarettes are treated in the same way as ordinary cigarettes and their use is prohibited on school premises',  Resources providing information and advice on the risks of vaping, designed to support parents, teachers and all those working with children and young people, are available on the PHA website at https://www.publichealth.hscni.net

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the consultation following the report on Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation closed in September 2024 following a publicity campaign across local and national media. We would like to thank all those who responded and attended one of the 16 public events, both in regional locations and online.  Following the closure of the consultation period, an analysis of the 269 responses was carried out with independent oversight from Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency.A draft report was then prepared and shared with members of the Consultation Forum in December to ensure that the terminology used is sensitive to victim and survivors. A copy was also provided to the TEO Committee.  We intend to publish the final version in coming weeks once final checks are completed. While we are pleased with the many areas of broad agreement highlighted in the consultation, further discussions with Executive Colleagues are needed on complex and sensitive policy issues before any final decisions can be made. It is also important for us to continue to engage and listen with victims and survivors in this period.We remain committed, along with the many members of this House, to help victims and their loved ones get the answers and support they deserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline his Department’s response to media reports of the presence of asbestos in school buildings.
answer_text: The Department manages asbestos in schools in accordance with the Control of Asbestos Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012.The Health and Safety Executive advises that if asbestos containing materials are in good condition, then they should be left undisturbed, and a system of management introduced. A system of management is in place in all school sectors to manage the risk to ensure that, as far as reasonably practicable, no one can come to harm from asbestos on the premises. The system of management includes monitoring the condition of, and keeping up to date records of, the condition and location of any known asbestos containing materials in the school.The risks are assessed and plans developed to manage those risks. If any deterioration of the materials occurs and removal, repair or encapsulation is required, this is carried out by a licensed asbestos company.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the £25 million childcare and early years investment has been used to finance the Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF); (ii) any work that has been undertaken with the Department of Health in the creation of the PSEISF; and (iii) whether the Department of Health has indicated that it will provide allied health support to children undergoing statutory assessment.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund is one of the package of measures funded from the £25m Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) budget for 2024/25.The establishment of the Fund was a proposal which emerged from the recently concluded End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).  Draft early intervention proposals emerging from the Review were shared with the Department of Health (DoH) at a joint Health/Education workshop in October 2024.  In addition, there was collaboration with the DoH to ensure alignment and avoid duplication with the Fair Play grant scheme.There has been no commitment from DoH to provide Allied Health Professional (AHP) support to children undergoing statutory assessment.  Pre-school settings in receipt of funding from the scheme may use it flexibly to meet the needs of the children in their settings, including for AHP support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how much of the £25 million childcare and early years investment was allocated to create additional childcare places for children with special educational needs; (ii) how many additional places have been created in this investment; (ii) any new pilot initiatives that have been created thus far using this funding; and (iii) whether the feedback has indicated that these pilots will be used in the draft Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: As part of the £25m for the Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy an additional £250k was allocated to the Department of Health to support the Fair Play Grant Scheme, bringing the total investment to £675k in 2024/25. This Scheme ensures children with additional needs are accessing quality play and learning experiences in registered community, voluntary and private provision throughout the academic year. While the Fair Play Scheme does not facilitate additional places, the total funding provided additional capacity to support a total of 87 settings which included 343 children in their care.  In addition, £1.5m has been allocated within my Department for a pilot Pre-school Education Inclusion Support Fund.  The Fund aims to provide resource to pre-school settings with children undergoing statutory assessment, to address the gap in support during this period. This Fund was launched on 26 November 2024 with 221 eligible settings receiving a Letter of Offer.Whilst feedback from pre-school settings to date has been very positive, inclusion in the longer-term ELC Strategy will be subject to evaluation of the pilot programme and the necessary budget being made available by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i), in order to assess the areas of objective need, whether his Department calculated the average deprivation points of the wider catchment area of each Sure Start provider or used the multiple deprivation measure to determine need based on the address of the Sure Start provider; (ii) to detail the formula used to determine the Sure Start providers that were eligible for the expansion; and (iii) for a comparable list of the average multiple deprivation points for each provider when (a) the average points of the catchment area is taken into account; and (b) whe
answer_text: Geographical areas within the DE Sure Start programme are determined by application of the Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure 2017 (NIMDM 2017) which is the official measure of spatial deprivation in Northern Ireland. At least the 25% most disadvantaged areas per NIMDM 2017 are covered by the programme and all children and families residing in postcodes in these areas can avail of Sure Start services, irrespective of the address of the Sure Start provider.DE applied NIMDM 2017 rankings to existing areas in Sure Start at the time of release of NIMDM 2017 and identified 22 SOAs that now fell within the 25% most disadvantaged per the new deprivation measure, but were not included in Sure Start. These 22 areas were brought into Sure Start on a permanent basis as part of the expansion of Sure Start in 2024/25. A recent independent review of the method for targeting Sure Start, concluded that continued use of the NIMDM was the most appropriate methodology to identify greatest need. The NIMDM rankings show the relative deprivation of one area to another, but not by how much more deprived.  Therefore, it would not be meaningful to produce an average of multiple deprivation measure ranks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the roll-out of the Pre-school Education Programme standardisation programme.
answer_text: The 2024/25 package of measures for parents and providers that I announced to the Assembly on 23rd May continue to be successfully implemented, including a major expansion of pre-school education provision.I announced at that time my intention that an additional 2,200 children would get a full-time pre-school place from September 2025, with more to follow in successive years.  My Department has now consulted on three tranches, comprising a total of 107 settings proposed to transition to the standardised session length from September 2025.  Subject to approval, this would represent over 2,500 additional children having access a full-time place from September 2025.  I also committed to make timely decisions on pre-school standardisation and indicated that I would endeavour to communicate my first decisions by the end of December.  I have already announced approval of 31 settings and published the details on the Departmental website.  Consultations on tranches two and three will end on 28 November and 13 December respectively and I am committed to ensuring that decisions on those settings are made as quickly as possible thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on her Department’s work to implement the Gillen Review Report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland, to support children and young people through the justice system.

To ask the Minister of Education, following the briefing at the Committee for Education on 6 November 2024 in relation to the Executive Childcare Strategy, to provide a breakdown of the (i) £23 million to be allocated in 2024-25; (ii) £55 million to be allocated in 2025-26; (iii) £80 million to be allocated in 2026-27; and (iv) £100 million to be allocated in 2027-28.
answer_text: My written statement of 23 May 2024 provided a breakdown of the £25m allocated for early learning and childcare measures in 2024/25.  You can read the full statement here: Written Ministerial Statement - Early Learning and Childcare Measures 2024/25 | Department of Education.The £55m, £80m and £100m bids for 2025/26, 2026-27 and 2027-28 represent the amounts required to continue the measures commenced in 2024/25 with some modest expansion.This is not the limit of my ambition for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  I intend to bring further costed recommendations for Executive consideration in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s ongoing work to promote early intervention practices in the childcare and early years sector to meet the needs of children showing early signs of special educational needs.
answer_text: The 2012 Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12 were developed by my department to promote the delivery of a consistent level of service quality and safety and to facilitate a consistent regional approach to registration and inspection. Those Standards stipulate that the inclusion, welfare, and development of children who have additional needs should actively be promoted, based on appropriate assessment.The Standards also require those in charge of childcare provision to sensitively discuss their observations with parents and encourage parents to seek relevant help, where a child in their care is identified as possibly having additional needs. Children’s rights to privacy and confidentiality must be respected when discussing potential additional needs, and providers must consult with parents about the child’s needs, including any special services or equipment for a child with additional needs. Indeed, before looking after a child with additional needs, the provider must ensure that the physical environment is suitable for that child. If a child is identified as a child in need, in accordance with Article 17 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (‘the Order’), the provider is required to, with the knowledge of the parents, give appropriate information to the referring agencies.Under Article 18 of the Order, Health and Social Care Trusts have a duty to provide a range and level of personal social care services to children who are considered to be children “in need” and their families. These include, among other services, childminding and day care provision. My department also manages Early Years Development Funding through the regional Childcare Partnership, which provides annual grants to support the wider development and quality improvement of the childcare sector. The Partnership facilitates an annual Fair Play Grant Scheme, which supports providers with additional financial support to meet the costs of providing care to children with additional needs. The department also administers the Sure Start programme on behalf of the Department of Education across Northern Ireland. Last year this programme was delivered through 38 projects to over 40,000 children. One of the programme’s key policy outcomes is the early identification of developmental delay. In the 2023/24 year Sure Start signposted or supported 10,301 service users to early intervention services in order to support families where children are showing early signs of specific support needs.My officials will continue to work closely with colleagues in Education and other departments to ensure that further work to improve the experience of children with additional needs and their families in early years service provision, under the auspices of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of whether there will be a significant resource implication for schools and other settings if his Department extends the requirement for formal education and training; and (ii) to detail the financial support his Department will providee schools who do not currently offer A-level exams in order to help those schools increase capacity.
answer_text: The detail of this proposal will be progressed over the coming months, and this will include assessing resource, capacity and funding requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a timeline of when the draft Childcare Strategy will be published.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to AQW 16775/22-27.  The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 18 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following his Ministerial Statement on 21 October 2024, whether early years professionalisation will be included in his investment to increase resources to support an effective and coherent approach to professional development.
answer_text: Early years and childcare workforce issues are being considered as part of the development of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  In conjunction with my Department, officials from the Department for the Economy, the Department of Health and the Department for Communities are exploring what actions might be possible in the context of a sector largely comprising private childcare businesses and self- employed childminders.  It is my intention to have a draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy ready for Executive consideration and public consultation in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the £25 million allocated for childcare and early years, whether any of the £7.1 million expansion of Early Years and Sure Start has been used to support the Belfast-wide Early Years Pilot Scheme.
answer_text: The Belfast Wide Early Years Pilot has not received funding from the 2024/25 budget allocation for early years and childcare.  This programme has been fully funded through the ‘A Fair Start’ budget since it commenced in 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the funding for the Belfast-wide Early Years pilot scheme will be extended beyond March 2025.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet set the 2025-26 DE Resource baseline budget and whilst no firm commitment can be given, I would hope to continue to fund the pilot until the end of the academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of (a) Sure Start providers; (b) Sure Start places; and (c) Sure Start staff, broken down by constituency; (ii) the average running cost of a Sure Start provider; and (iii) the average projected cost per child at Sure Start.
answer_text: As outlined in my response to AQW 16066/22-27, there are 38 Sure Start projects across Northern Ireland. Sure Start services are available to around 41,500 children and their families.  The table below shows the numbers of Sure Start staff broken down by constituency, as advised by the Department of Health (DoH) which administers the programme on behalf of my Department.ConstituencySure Start - Staff Numbers Belfast East37Belfast North88Belfast South and Mid Down50Belfast West164East Antrim31East Londonderry83Fermanagh and South Tyrone77Foyle113Lagan Valley23Mid Ulster53Newry and Armagh88North Antrim71North Down18South Down46Strangford29Upper Bann62West Tyrone124Total1157Sure Start funding is not allocated on cost per child basis, rather it is determined by the cost of delivering services to meet the needs of individual Sure Start project areas.  These costs are determined by local Sure Start management and the DoH. The average Sure Start project allocation in 2024-25 is £863,065.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to provide additional support to the community and voluntary childcare and early years sector.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQO/923/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any plans his Department has to include additional childcare provision in the anti-poverty strategy; and (ii) whether he will commit to including provision for those on Universal Credit.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaging with officials from other Departments including Education to discuss the development of the Strategy and the delivery of a range of keystone actions which will help mitigate impacts, reduce the risk of falling into poverty and support all people including children to exit poverty. I will ensure that the work of my Department on developing the broader Anti-Poverty Strategy aligns with and supports the Department of Education’s work on the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.There is currently provision to help working parents who are in receipt of Universal Credit with the costs of childcare. The Childcare Costs Element of Universal Credit pays for up to 85% of eligible childcare costs and is additional to the monthly standard allowance. The Childcare Costs Element is currently £1,014.63 for one child and £1,739.37 for two or more children per monthly assessment period. This is paid in arrears.Since October 2021, the Department has provided assistance with the payment of upfront childcare costs.  A payment of up to £1,500 is available through the Adviser Discretion Fund. This will be paid directly to the registered childcare provider and will not impact entitlement to Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to provide additional resources to provide summer schemes for those children with special educational needs who have not yet started school to ensure they make up for missed time.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) recently commissioned an exercise to identify budget requirements for the periods 2025/26 to 2027/28, although this exercise will only provide a one year budget for 2025/26.  My department has submitted its return and is awaiting the outcome of this exercise. Similar to recent financial years, I anticipate a significant resource funding gap next year and beyond.  To this end, any additional resources necessary to provide summer schemes for our children with special educational needs (SEN) will need to be considered alongside other significant funding pressures the department will face in 2025/26.The Education Authority (EA) have advised that there are contingency plans in place for all children who have been affected by delays to school start dates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with special educational needs have not yet started school (i) in total; (ii) due to work on their new Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools not being completed; and (iii) that are placed in a school in South Down.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised the following:in total 127 children have not yet started school and all have bespoke contingency arrangements in place;13 of these children are impacted by works on their new Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiM) not yet being completed, and all have bespoke contingency arrangements in place; no pupils are impacted in the South Down area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools that are not yet completed; and (ii) the reasons these are not yet completed.
answer_text: 112 new specialist provision in mainstream classes were established in 105 schools for the 2024/25 academic year.  Of these new provisions, works are ongoing to complete 28 classes.The reasons why works are not complete are specific to each project but include the timing of the initiation of the project when pupil placements were confirmed, contractor capacity, supply chain issues, change in scope of the works and the receipt of the relevant statutory approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to (i) increase the number of Irish language pre-school, early years and childcare places; and (ii) resource the Irish language pre-school, early years and childcare sector by funding the production of age appropriate learning resources that are suitable for children aged between 0-4 who speak Irish.
answer_text: My Department aims to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target-aged child whose parents want it.  Registered Irish-medium pre-school education providers with sufficient children can be funded under the Department’s Pre-School Education Programme even where sufficient English-medium provision is available in the area.My Department has funded Altram to develop and pilot an Irish-Medium Pre-school Immersion Education accredited course for Northern Ireland and to provide the Irish-Medium Pre-school Support Service. My Department’s Sure Start programme is accessible to all children aged 0-3 years and their families living in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland.  Services delivered through the medium of Irish are determined by management of the 38 Sure Start projects, taking account of local circumstances and need.  My Department’s Pathway Fund provides additional targeted support to children up to age four who are at risk of not reaching their full educational potential. All 14 Irish Medium projects which applied and met eligibility requirements are in receipt of an award in 2024-25, totalling £277,456.  I made a Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 advising Members of a £25m package of measures for early learning and childcare in 2024/25. The package included commencement of the standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme which, in time, will provide all children with at least 22.5 hours funded provision in the year before they start primary school, this includes the Irish-Medium sector.    The measures currently being implemented will be evaluated to inform the development of future proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the allocation of the 100 pre-school settings for the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) for September 2025; (ii) the criteria used to choose the first 100 pre-school settings to benefit from the PSEP; (iii) the number of targeted PSEP places, broken down by constituency; (iv) the number of children his Department is seeking to target thought the PSEP scheme, broken down by constituency; and (v) the number Irish language places that will be created through the PSEP scheme.
answer_text: It is not yet possible to advise which individual pre-school settings will transition in September 2025. The EA will need to assess the state of readiness of each pre-school setting before putting them forward for approval to transform to deliver the longer session length. The Education Authority (EA) will contact settings in due course about this. All settings had the opportunity to provide initial advice on their self-assessed state of readiness during the Standardisation Scoping Study which is available on the DE website at Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk). This information will be used to inform planning and the initial engagement with the EA.Further information on the Standardisation Programme is available on the DE website at Pre-School Education Programme - Standardisation | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk) and this will be updated as the programme progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the rationale for changing the Social Economy criteria on Pathway Funding; and (ii) for a comparison of the current criteria and the previous criteria for the Pathway Fund on their effectiveness at tackling disadvantage and poverty.
answer_text: Pathway Fund applications initially received an enhanced score if applicant settings were operating as, or working towards becoming, a Social Economy Model (SEM). However, feedback from the sector conveyed that operating as a SEM required charging of fees which was not feasible in areas of disadvantage where the Pathway Fund is targeted. In 2022/23 the SEM criterion was therefore revised to one which focused on the enhancement of existing services through improving quality, staff development, partnerships, parental engagement, management and governance.The revised criterion enables Pathway projects to demonstrate how the activities and training they undertake contribute to the sustainability of their setting, with a greater focus on how they can improve the lives of children that are supported through their projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the expansion of Sure Start provisions to the 22 most disadvantaged areas under the Executive's Early Learning and Childcare Strategy; (ii) the criteria used to choose the 22 most disadvantaged areas; (iii) the targeted Sure Start places, broken down by constituency; (iv) the number of children his Department is seeking to target through the expansion of Sure Start, broken down by constituency; and (v) the number of Irish language places that will be created through the expansion of Sure Start provisions.
answer_text: Additional funding made available by the Executive this year of £25m for early learning and childcare, has enabled me to increase the 2024-25 Sure Start budget allocation by £3.1m (an uplift of 10% on the 2023-24 funding level). The funding has enabled expansion of provision by bringing 22 areas into Sure Start on a permanent basis.These 22 areas were identified as falling within the 25% most disadvantaged areas when applying the latest deprivation measure, the Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure (NIMDM) 2017).  Their inclusion enables provision of the policy objective of providing Sure Start in the 25% most disadvantaged areas of Northern Ireland.The table below provides details of the 22 targeted areas and numbers of eligible children aged 0-3 years in the areas, broken down by constituency, as advised by the Department of Health (programme administrator).Sure Start Project Super Output AreaEligible Children Aged 0-3 YearsConstituencyEast BelfastBloomfield 3107Belfast EastSmile Sure StartBellevue 2118Belfast NorthAbbey Sure StartCarnmoney 161South AntrimAbbey Sure StartGlengormley 270Belfast NorthAbbey Sure StartMossley 297South AntrimColeraine Sure StartAtlantic102East LondonderryDalriada Sure StartRoute59North AntrimGold Sure StartTown Parks East 285Mid UlsterHorizon Sure StartCentral (Larne)59East AntrimBangor Sure StartDonaghadee South 158North DownArds Sure StartGlen 1100StrangfordLisburn Sure StartHilden 196Lagan ValleyLisburn Sure StartHilden 266Lagan ValleyArke Sure StartDerrynoose267Newry & ArmaghMourne Sure StartAnnalong 2101South DownNewry City Sure StartClonallan 1108South DownSouth Armagh Sure StartForkhill 2175Newry & ArmaghCherish Sure StartErne127Fermanagh & South TyroneCherish Sure StartLisnaskea162Fermanagh & South TyroneCherish Sure StartPortora180Fermanagh & South TyroneRainbow Sure StartBelleek and Boa139Fermanagh & South TyroneWaterside Sure StartAltnagelvin_1101Foyle Total  22 Areas 2,438Sure Start services delivered through the medium of Irish are determined by management of the 38 Sure Start projects across Northern Ireland, taking account of local circumstances and need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his plans to provide additional support to the community and voluntary childcare and early years sector.
answer_text: Voluntary & community groups play an important role in providing services and support to children and their families.My Department’s Women’s Centre Childcare Fund currently provides funding to 13 Women’s Centres across Northern Ireland, enabling access to affordable or free childcare sessions to help parents access employment and training opportunities.  This is provided as short, two-hour sessions which provide flexibility for women to access services.In the current financial year, I have allocated a 5% uplift across my Department’s Voluntary & Community Sector funding programmes, bringing the total funding awarded to the Women’s Centre Childcare Fund to almost £881k for 2024/25.The financial constraints currently faced by my Department do not provide scope for any new or additional programmes to support the Voluntary & Community childcare and early years sector at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's input into the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on plans to assist Members to improve personal security across all constituency offices.
To ask the Minister for Communities what support his Department is providing to the development of the childcare and early years strategy.
answer_text: Ensuring the availability of appropriate and affordable childcare that meets the needs of parents and children will be a vital aspect of the Executive’s work to address both poverty and gender equality and will be a key aspect of the wider Anti-Poverty and Gender Equality Strategies. I am, therefore, very supportive of the broader Childcare Strategy and believe that our Departments’ work in these areas should be closely linked and complementary.Senior officials from my Department have participated on the Northern Ireland Childcare Affordability Scheme Task and Finish Group. As this work progresses, I will keep my Department’s commitments under review to ensure that they remain supportive of the broader Childcare Strategy’s objectives. For example, my officials shared details of the existing financial support Universal Credit provides to help eligible parents start and stay in work.  This includes help with upfront childcare from our Adviser Discretion fund and help with up to 85% of ongoing childcare costs. A key challenge for the childcare and early years strategy is the supply of trained and fully assessed childminders and my Department funds Labour Market Partnerships (LMPs) to address challenges in the local labour market. LMPs have already provided 133 childminding training places and my officials have been engaging with colleagues in the Department of Education to discuss the potential for continuing to utilise LMP provision for training child minders to support the Strategy.I and my Executive colleagues will seek to ensure alignment of approach between the strategies in addressing the issues faced by children and their parents, with a view to ensuring all parents who want to work are enabled to do so.

To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the number of (a) primary schools; and (b) post-primary schools that requested the careers guidance service from his Department in the 2022-23 academic year; and (ii) for a breakdown of the post-primary schools by (a) grammar; and (b) other.
answer_text: (i) During the 2022-23 academic year, my Department’s Careers Service:	(a) received no requests for services from primary schools.  Careers provision in Primary Schools does not fall within the remit of DfE Careers Service.	(b) received requests for and delivered careers guidance services to 217 post primary schools across Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has given any consideration to providing additional support to school principals to address the increasing use of vapes in schools.
answer_text: The issue of vaping is primarily a public health matter and in certain circumstances, a criminal justice matter.  As such, my Department will follow appropriate guidance from the Department of Health/Public Health Agency (PHA) and Department of Justice/Police Service Northern Ireland (PSNI) on this issue.  As part of the statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding at primary level and Learning for Life and Work, Personal Development strand at post-primary level – schools have a duty to deliver drugs education (including legal and illegal substances).  This should raise awareness amongst children of the risks / dangers associated with vaping.Education will continue to address issues such as this as part of the statutory curriculum.  At post primary level (Key Stage 3), schools are required to provide learning opportunities for pupils to investigate the effects on the body of legal and illegal substances and the risks and consequences of their misuse. Schools have the flexibility to work directly with the PHA, PSNI and other organisations to provide learning opportunities in support of delivery of the curriculum.Furthermore, there is a statutory duty on schools to both promote positive behaviour and have a Positive Behaviour policy in place as well as an associated Drugs policy.  The Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessments has published drugs guidance for schools, developed in consultation with the PHA and South-Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, which covers vaping (noted in the guidance as E-cigarettes).  This guidance is available at: Drugs Education - Revised Guidance.  A school’s safeguarding and child protection policy should also link to their drugs policy and include the requirement to notify the PSNI in relation to any incidents relating to illegal substances.Problems associated with vaping are reflective of wider societal issues that need the support of community and other organisations outside of education settings working together to support young people at risk through vaping.  There is a range of public health and voluntary and community groups which may be able to provide help and advice to young people and to parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the work his Department has carried out in relation to mental health and well-being in early years and preschool.
answer_text: Fostering children’s mental health and well-being in their early years is crucial for their overall development.  Programmes such as Sure Start, the Pre-school Education Programme and Getting Ready to Learn (GRtL), part of my Department’s £100m plus annual investment in Early Years, are key to delivering such support.Through collaborative delivery with the Department of Health, the Sure Start programme promotes children’s and parents’ mental health and well-being, by providing comprehensive support for families and young children, focusing on early learning, health and family support services. The pre-school curricular guidance, which all funded pre-school providers are required to adhere to, sets Personal, Social and Emotional Development as one of the six areas of learning to support children’s emotional well-being.The GRtL Programme is available to all funded pre-school settings; through the ‘Happy Healthy Kids’ theme, parents are provided with information and ideas for supporting their child’s physical and emotional well-being at home.  I am pleased to have secured £25m funding to implement a package of additional Early Learning and Childcare measures in 2024/25.  The funding will allow for stabilisation and expansion of vital early years provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the work his Department has carried out in relation to bullying in early years and preschool.
answer_text: The pre-school curricular guidance sets Personal, Social and Emotional Development as one of the six areas of learning. This area of learning is about children’s emotional well-being, understanding of who they are, and respect for others and their environment.The legal definition of bullying type behaviour indicates that the school must determine that the children involved have demonstrated intent, the capacity to target, and the repeated presentation of socially unacceptable behaviour. For this to qualify as bullying type behaviour, staff must conclude that the child is intentionally meaning to cause psychological and/or physical harm, which is rarely appropriate to determine at this young developmental age. The Department funds the Education Authority’s Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) to support schools to successfully implement the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  ABSIT have worked on Addressing Bullying Behaviour Policies with a limited number of Early Years Settings and have also dealt with a small number of referrals where the parents of preschool aged children have contacted the nursery or play group alleging that their child had experienced bullying type behaviour. In these instances, ABSIT worked with the staff in the context of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act to ensure that the appropriate advice on scaffolding, assessment and support was provided to meet the needs of the children, parent/carer, and staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what financial support is provided to GPs to address increasing demand in services.
answer_text: I have already indicated that as Minister, one of my immediate priority areas, subject to available resources, is enhancing primary care.  I was pleased to announce recently that the 2024/25 GMS contract has been agreed. A key aim of the new 2024/25 contract is to provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices.To support this, the 2024/25 contract brings the Quality and Outcomes Framework and specified Enhanced Services into the core contract and under the Clinical Care Domain of a new Contract Assurance Framework.  The associated funding is incorporated into the core contract.  In addition, funding that is provided for clinical waste will also be repurposed into core GMS funding.As a result of these actions, a total of £38.9million will be made available for re-purposing.Of this, £5million will be dedicated to GP Indemnity, as an interim measure pending identification of the long-term model for future provision.  The remaining £33.9million will be added to core Global Sum payments made to practices.Over the past number of years, my Department has made significant additional investment to build capacity in Primary Care through initiatives such as the Primary Care MDT Programme, and the General Practice Pharmacist programme.  These programmes represent recurrent additional investment of £25m and £19.6m per annum, respectively.  My Department has also previously made available significant funding to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.Unfortunately, the 2024/25 Budget, recently approved by the Executive, was extremely disappointing for my Department and this is likely to have serious consequences on how I can further support critical areas such as primary care.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of learning support units currently operating in (i) primary schools; (ii) post-primary schools; and (iii) schools in the South Down constituency.
answer_text: There are currently 28 specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPiMS) operating in primary schools in the South Down constituency.There are currently 14 SPiMS operating in post-primary schools in the South Down constituency.There are currently 42 SPiMS across 18 schools in the South Down constituency.Some of these SPiMS were previously referred to as ‘learning support units’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider offering financial support to schools to provide transport to and from extra circular activities, such as all-ability swimming lessons for children with special educational needs.
answer_text: Schools receive delegated budgets through the Common Funding Scheme to enable Boards of Governors and Principals to use resources in accordance with individual schools’ needs and priorities, including effective delivery of the curriculum.  Each school’s delegated budget share is expected to meet a range of items of expenditure, including transport of pupils (other than home to school transport).  It is therefore at each school’s discretion to determine how much, if any, of its funding is attributed to such activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to issue an audit on all current specialist provisions in mainstream schools to identify gaps in funding, resources and training.
answer_text: On 23 April 2024, the Permanent Secretary wrote to all schools encouraging them to consider establishing specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) classes, after which on 24 April 2024 a short survey issued which invited schools to detail what provision existed currently and allowed schools to comment on any additional requirements (including funding, resources or training) either to maintain existing provision or what would be needed to facilitate new provision.The survey closed on 3 May 2024, with analysis of these findings to be completed and the results of which will be used to inform actions going forward.Furthermore, the Education Training Inspectorate (ET) will, before the end of the academic year, publish its report on SPiMS classes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to (i) launch a flood support fund for farmers in South Down who have suffered negative consequences due to flooding in November 2023 and the erratic weather patterns that has followed; and (ii) assist farmers recover costs of lost crop yield and grazing pastures due to large bodies of water from the flooding and erratic weather patterns.
answer_text: I can confirm that there are presently no plans to implement a financial support scheme for farmers affected by last year's flooding. 		The case for a financial support scheme for potato growers affected by flooding in counties Armagh and Down has been rigorously examined by DAERA. A business case was developed to test the need for and value for money (including the net additional NI economy impact) of a potential support package. However, the conclusion of this process was that government intervention was not likely to meet the threshold associated with managing of public money required in order to justify and authorise this expenditure. For this reason, DAERA has not implemented a support scheme. 		There were further on-the-ground inspections during March to determine the latest position but this did not support a change in the conclusion. 		DAERA recognises that 2023 was a challenging year for potato growers and the very wet and late spring of 2024 is now negatively impacting farming operations across almost every sector within the industry. 		I am conscious of wider impact if wet weather continues in the time ahead and I have made representations to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention if the situation lasts for much longer.Meanwhile, my officials in the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) continue to monitor the situation very closely, maintaining contact with stakeholders and farmers across the range of sectors within the industry. CAFRE has released a number of advisory articles on the management of farming operations following this period of prolonged inclement weather and tailored advice is also being offered at group meetings and through one-to-one discussions with individual farmers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) children that require a special educational needs (SEN) provision place in September 2024; (ii) children that require a SEN provision place in the Downpatrick area in September 2024; (iii) schools that have been asked to take a SEN provision place in September 2024; (iv) schools that have been asked to take a SEN provision place in the Downpatrick area in September 2024; and (v) schools that have responded positively to providing SEN provision places in the Downpatrick area in September 2024.
answer_text: Latest Education Authority (EA) planning assumptions indicate that almost 5,800 children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) will require either a new or change of placement for September 2024, based on their statement of SEN or annual review.   This figure changes frequently as children are continually flowing through the statementing and review process.The EA collates data on SEN placements by Local Government District (LGD) area.  Newry, Mourne and Down has been identified by the EA as an area of particular pressure with additional places required in both special schools and specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPiMS) classes.  All schools have been asked to consider their capacity to establish new or additional specialist education provision for September 2024.  The EA, supported by education stakeholders, wrote to all schools on 12 February 2024.  Further to this, on 23 April 2024 the Permanent Secretary issued a letter to all nursery, primary and post-primary schools in Northern Ireland encouraging them to consider establishing new specialist provision or expanding existing provision.  This letter was followed by a short survey to determine existing or new capacity which issued on 24 April 2024 and closes on 3 May 2024.  The initial response rate to the survey has been very positive and the results will be considered and followed up by the department and the EA, with the support of education stakeholders, to determine options moving forward.	The EA, supported by my department, is working at pace to engage with all education sectors in the area to build capacity and address need to ensure that all children have a suitable education placement by September 2024.  The position is fluid and changes daily as discussions with schools are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress of the School Street Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing an Active Travel Delivery Plan, including a full update of our active travel design guidance. The new guidance is in the process of being finalised and is expected to be introduced later this year.When complete, the Active Travel Delivery Plan will provide our teams with a firm basis for the assessment, prioritisation and design of high-quality active travel infrastructure within and connecting our towns and cities. The plan will include measures to encourage safer active travel journeys to schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the improvements being made through the Planning Improvement Programme.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way.Good progress has already been made, including the implementation of a planning fee inflationary uplift and a proposed second uplift for this financial year; the progression of work to bring forward arrangements for statutory validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications entering the system; the launch (December 2023) of a public consultation into a fundamental review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015; and a review of the internal administration of the notification process.I am reviewing progress with my officials and working closely with key stakeholders to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system. Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the bespoke training programme being developed in the absence of team teacher training for staff in specialist provision in mainstream settings; and (ii) whether this will be ready to roll-out to staff this year.
answer_text: Team Teach training has been provided to special schools (via the Education Authority (EA)) since the introduction of the department’s Use of Reasonable Force Guidance Circular 1999/09 - Use of reasonable force (education-ni.gov.uk). It is important to note that Team Teach is a commercial business and accredited provider of positive behaviour management training, equipping individuals and teams in a variety of settings to deal with challenging situations and behaviours in ways that should lead to desirable outcomes and positive relationships.  The training operates on a number of tiers and focuses on the use of de-escalation techniques to help staff defuse problem situations and also trains some staff, where appropriate, in the application of hold techniques which may be necessary to use for an individual for either their own safety or the safety of others within the setting. In answer to your specific questions in relation to development of training programmes for staff in specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPiMS):The EA Behaviour Support Service provides similar training to Team Teach, including in de-escalation techniques, conflict management, restorative practice and the legislative aspect relative to Northern Ireland, which is available to all schools. The EA advise it is currently developing a revised training package for delivery as a pilot to those schools with specialist settings called: ‘Whole School Approach to Prevent, Relate, Support and Respond to Behaviours that Require Intervention’.  Pending the outcome of the pilot, it is envisaged that the training will be rolled out across all settings.The EA’s Specialist Setting Support Team (SSST) also provides support and a broad range of training opportunities to staff specifically in newly established SPiMS.  Subject to additional budget availability, the EA proposes to expand the reach of the SSST to include all SPiMS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he has any plans to introduce homeless prevention legislation in this mandate, similar to that in England and the South of Ireland; and (ii) what action has been taken to date on this.
answer_text: Addressing homelessness is a priority for me and my Department. I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on the implementation of the Adult Restorative Justice Strategy Action Plan.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Adult Restorative Justice Strategy in March 2022, my officials have been working with key partners to deliver on the commitments outlined in the Strategy’s five-year Action Plan. To date, good progress has been made across a number of actions, with the most significant being the following: A key action for Year 1 of the Action Plan was to review and refresh the 2007 Protocol, which governs the use of restorative approaches in the criminal justice system.  Having appointed an independent panel of experts to conduct this review, I signed off on their report which was published in February 2023, and the new agreed Protocol was published in July 2023. One of the key recommendations in the panel’s report was the appointment of a full-time independent person as an Interim Protocol Lead (IPL).  I am pleased to say that an appointment was made on 20 February 2024.  The IPL will play a critical role in progressing the new arrangements set out in the revised Protocol, including setting training standards and designing a new accreditation process.  This work will be key to enabling the increased use of Restorative Justice as an integral part of the justice system and in the ongoing delivery of the Strategy’s Action Plan.   Finally, in line with our commitment in the Strategy to explore restorative diversionary approaches for adults, this month our PSNI partners have launched a pilot scheme in North and West Belfast which incorporates a restorative element within Community Resolution Notices for certain low-level offences for first time offenders.   Progress made in implementing the Strategy and Action Plan is reported by my Department annually.  The first Annual Report was published in July 2023, and my officials are currently working to develop the second such report.  The multi-agency Restorative Justice Working Group, which holds overarching responsibility for the Strategy, is currently looking to agree the Year 3 actions and planning their delivery, alongside progressing ongoing actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when a review of the Education Authority's School Suicide Prevention Strategy will take place.
answer_text: The Education Authority does not have a School Suicide Prevention Strategy.  The Department and EA do engage with health colleagues on their Protect Life 2 – Suicide Prevention Strategy.  This is a long-term strategy for reducing suicides and the incidence of self-harm with action delivered across a range of Government departments, agencies, and sectors.  The Education Authority supports the work of this strategy through a range of delivery mechanisms as well as advice and support to schools (this list is not exhaustive), for example:The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS)Critical Incident Response Team (CIRT)Youth Service REACH programBeing Well Doing Well: A Whole School Approach to Emotional Health and WellbeingA range of emotional health and wellbeing training opportunities for schools, including Connections training, which specifically relates to suicide prevention.EOTAS (Education Other Than at School)Child Protection Support ServiceEducation Welfare ServiceEducational Psychology Service

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many cases on the Housing Executive waiting list in South Down are (i) classified as homeless; (ii) awaiting temporary accommodation; and (iii) currently in temporary accommodation and awaiting permanent accommodation.
answer_text: (i) The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin July - December 2023 contains information on the numbers classified as experiencing homelessness and is available here:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2023 It should be noted that it is not possible to provide any data beyond 31 December until the publication of the next bulletin which is scheduled for June 2024.(ii) and (iii) of your question is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(ii) The provision of temporary accommodation is a statutory duty as per the Housing (NI) order 1988. In such circumstances, any household requiring temporary accommodation will be offered temporary accommodation as per the statutory duty outlined in the Housing (NI) Order. (iii) As of 31 December 2023, there was a total of 225 households within the Newry, Mourne and Down district council area who were placed in temporary accommodation and awaiting permanent accommodation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the Environment Agency has raised concerns with the water quality of the River Quoile in Downpatrick; and (ii) to detail his plans to examine the water quality, maintenance and provisions for the River Quoile.
answer_text: With any water body that is not at ‘good’ status, NIEA has concerns regarding the water quality. The ecological status for the Quoile River water body was assessed as moderate in the 2021 classification mainly due to agricultural and waste water pressures. 	The work required is summarised in the draft third River Basin Management Plan for Northern Ireland. Following my consideration and review in light of the challenges posed by the occurrence of blue-green algal blooms in Lough Neagh, I will bring the Plan to the Executive for consideration in due course. Under the terms of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973, the Department for Infrastructure inspects and maintains the Quoile River from a flood risk perspective. This involves liaison with DAERA and public advertisement in advance of programmed maintenance works.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the costed options for the childcare strategy; and (ii) when these options will be presented to the stakeholder engagement group.
answer_text: My Department convened a meeting of the Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Stakeholder Engagement Forum (SEF) in December 2023 and presented a range of potential actions for inclusion within an ELC Strategy and their indicative costs.  The work has evolved since then and we are in the process of further refining the options in terms of both short and longer-term measures.  Over the coming weeks, I am scheduled to meet with the SEF, parents and others with an interest in the strategy, to hear their views firsthand.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the review of Minimum Standards for Childcare; and (ii) when this will be presented to the Assembly.
answer_text: The review will commence in the coming weeks with a consultation on the issues of pre-employment health declarations and adult:child ratios. Childcare is a priority area for the wider Executive, and the review will be an important part of ongoing work across Departments to deliver improvements for childcare providers and users. Updates will be provided to the Assembly at the appropriate points in time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans the Driver and Vehicle Agency has to modify existing (i) services; (ii) facilities; and (iii) staffing arrangements, in South Down.
answer_text: The DVA has no immediate plans to modify existing services and facilities at Downpatrick test centre. The DVA will continue to deliver vehicle test and driving test services from Downpatrick test centre and is in the process of recruiting both vehicle examiners and driving examiners with a view to filling staff vacancies across its network of test centres, including Downpatrick.The DVA is investing in new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk, which are due to open in 2024 and 2025 respectively.   Further investment in new test centres will be subject to further consideration and funding availability.The new test centre at Hydebank will be the first test centre to be fitted out with new vehicle testing equipment.   Thereafter, the DVA will agree a programme to roll out new equipment to all existing test centres.
